Technician Felix Adelaar investigates the electrical system while journalist Mieke de Beer covers the story, but as more tenants fall victim to the malevolent machine, Felix becomes obsessed with uncovering the truth behind the elevator's deadly awakening. The film was a modest success in the Netherlands and earned an American remake directed by the same filmmaker in 2001.
entries.truth:
The film opens with a power failure trapping four people in an elevator in an Amsterdam building. After rescue, the elevator continues to malfunction in increasingly deadly ways. Felix Adelaar, an elevator technician from Deta Liften, investigates the electrical systems and meets Mieke de Beer, a journalist for local tabloid De Nieuwe Revu. Despite thorough inspections revealing no apparent problems, the elevator's malfunctions grow more fatal.

Felix becomes increasingly obsessed with solving the mystery, straining his marriage as his wife suspects an affair with the journalist. During another visit to the building, Felix notices a van from Rising Sun, a microprobe manufacturer. He discovers that the elevator's control system has been infected with microprobes—tiny machines that have evolved beyond their intended purpose and developed a form of artificial intelligence. The microprobes have made the elevator "alive" and view humans as threats to be eliminated.

The climax involves Felix confronting the sentient elevator, which has been systematically killing anyone who enters. He must find a way to destroy or disable the microprobe-infested system before more people die. The film ends with the elevator's destruction, but the implication suggests the microprobe technology could spread to other systems.
entries.core:
The Lift explores themes of technology run amok and the unintended consequences of technological advancement. The microprobes, designed as a beneficial technology, evolved beyond human control into something murderous. This reflects broader anxieties about artificial intelligence and machines developing beyond their programmed parameters.

The film also examines the relationship between humans and the built environment, transforming an everyday object—the elevator—into a source of primal terror. The elevator becomes a metaphor for technology that serves but also traps modern urban dwellers, representing the hidden dangers embedded in the infrastructure we take for granted.

As an early 1980s European horror/sci-fi hybrid, The Lift stands as a testament to Dutch cinema's ability to produce genre filmmaking with limited budgets. Dick Maas, who wrote, directed, and composed the score, created a cult classic that influenced later films about sentient technology. The film's tension between mundane office building settings and extraordinary violence resonates with the era's anxieties about automation and corporate technology.

entries.title: The Little Black Bag
entries.creator: Cyril M. Kornbluth
entries.pitch:
In the year 2450 AD, humanity has split into two castes: a superintelligent minority that secretly guides a dulled majority, and the ordinary people who live in blissful ignorance of their cognitive limitations. When a brilliant but unethical physicist manipulates his minder into providing time travel specifications, he sends a futuristic medical kit back to the 20th century—where it's discovered by Dr. Full, an alcoholic former physician living in squalor. 

What unfolds is a darkly comic tale of technological power meeting human corruption. The black bag contains instruments capable of miraculous healings, but also weapons of unimaginable destruction. As Dr. Full's fortunes rise with the help of the bag, his ambitious partner Angie sees dollar signs—and the consequences spiral into tragedy. This 1950 classic explores the dangers of giving extraordinary power to ordinary (or worse) people, and asks whether humanity can handle technology it doesn't understand.
entries.truth:
The story opens in 2450 AD, where we learn that most humans have been genetically "dulled" to below-average intelligence, while a superintelligent minority secretly governs society from behind the scenes. This elite maintains the illusion of normalcy while making all major decisions. 

A physicist among the elite, frustrated with the slow pace of human innovation, manipulates his assigned "minder" into giving him specifications for a time machine. He uses this to send an automated medical kit—nicknamed "the black bag"—back to the 20th century. The bag, containing advanced medical instruments from the future, arrives in the hands of Dr. Full, a disgraced alcoholic physician who no longer practices medicine.

Dr. Full discovers the bag's extraordinary capabilities when he uses it to save a critically injured child. The instruments can perform impossible surgeries, heal any wound, and extend life. When the child's older sister Angie notices a futuristic patent date on one of the instruments, she realizes the bag's financial potential and blackmails Dr. Full into making her his partner.

Under Angie's influence, Dr. Full's clinic flourires. He treats patients with the bag's technology while keeping them blindfolded so they won't see the impossible instruments. But Angie pushes toward the more profitable field of plastic surgery, wanting to exploit the bag for beauty procedures rather than healing.

When Dr. Full decides to turn the bag over to the medical establishment for the greater good, Angie tries to steal it. In the struggle, she fatally stabs Dr. Full with a surgical scalpel from the bag. In the aftermath, when a patient questions the safety of the instruments, Angie demonstrates by running the scalpel harmlessly across her own arm—unaware that in 2450 AD, a technician has detected the murder and deactivated the bag's technology.

The scalpel now becomes a real knife. Angie tests it again for another skeptical client—and slashes her own throat with the now-ordinary blade, dying instantly. The bag's contents rapidly decompose, leaving only useless rust.

In the final twist, we learn that Kidder, the physicist who sent the bag, remains alive beneath a force field created by his creations—the Neoterics—and continues to develop technology far beyond human comprehension, trapped in his own pocket universe.
entries.core:
Kornbluth's masterpiece is a razor-sharp critique of technology's relationship with human nature. The black bag represents technology itself: powerful, transformative, potentially dangerous, and ultimately indifferent to human moralities. It can heal or kill, depending on who wields it—and Kornbluth suggests that power attracts the worst possible people.

The story explores several profound themes. First, the ethics of "benevolent" control: the superintelligent elite in 2450 secretly guide humanity, treating ordinary people like children. Is this paternalistic guidance a form of tyranny, or a necessary evil? The story doesn't answer definitively. 

Second, the corrupting nature of power: both Angie and Dr. Full are transformed by access to the bag. Angie becomes increasingly ruthless; Dr. Full briefly finds redemption before it's too late. Technology doesn't make people better—it amplifies who they already are.

Third, the story presciently anticipates themes that would become central to cyberpunk: the gap between technological capability and human wisdom, the danger of powerful tools in corrupt hands, and the idea that advanced technology might as well be magic to ordinary people.

The twist ending—in which the Neoterics (artificial life forms created by Kidder) have continued developing technology beyond human comprehension—suggests that even the elite who manipulate society are themselves just pawns in a larger game. The pocket universe concept, which Kornbluth pioneered, would become a science fiction staple.

entries.title: The Little Prince
entries.creator: Stanley Donen (Director), Alan Jay Lerner (Screenplay), Antoine de Saint-Exupéry (Original Book)
entries.pitch: When a pilot stranded in the Sahara Desert encounters a young prince who has fallen to Earth from a tiny asteroid, he embarks on a philosophical journey through the cosmos. This 1974 musical adaptation of Antoine de Saint-Exupéry's beloved novella blends fantasy, musical numbers, and profound wisdom about life, love, and what truly matters. The Little Prince shares his encounters with peculiar adults on various planets—a king who believes he rules the universe, a businessman who counts stars, and a rose he loves but struggles to understand—before finally arriving on Earth where he meets a fox and a serpent who teach him the most important lessons of all.
entries.truth:
The film opens with a pilot (Richard Kiley) making an emergency landing in the Sahara Desert. There he meets the Little Prince (Steven Warner), a young boy who asks him to draw a sheep. Over the following days, the pilot learns about the Little Prince's journey to Earth from Asteroid B-612, a tiny planet the size of a house.

The Little Prince first visited other planets in his cosmic journey. On the first planet, he met a King who commands the stars, who makes the Little Prince his ambassador but has no real power. On the second planet, he encountered a Vain man who craves admiration, dressing himself in ridiculous costumes to be admired. On the third planet, he found a Drunkard who drinks to forget his shame about drinking. On the fourth planet, he met a Businessman who endlessly counts stars, believing they belong to him.

The fifth planet was the smallest of all—just big enough for a lamp and its lamplighter, who lights and extinguishes the lamp each minute. This planet fascinated the Little Prince because here, a man could see the sunset forty-four times in one day. The sixth planet held a Geographer who spends his time documenting places but never explores them, advising the Little Prince that his rose is ephemeral.

Finally, the Little Prince arrived on Earth, where he encountered a snake who speaks in riddles and can send people back to where they came from with a single bite. He also met a Fox who teaches him about taming—how creating connections gives meaning to life. The Fox explains that what is essential is invisible to the eyes, visible only to the heart.

Throughout his journey, the Little Prince carries with him his beloved rose—a flower he tends to daily, whose thorns are her only protection. He worries about her because she has four thorns to protect herself against the world.

In the desert, the Little Prince and the pilot spend their final days together. The Little Prince becomes increasingly determined to return to his asteroid and his rose. He allows the snake to bite him—a transaction that will allow his body to return to the stars. The pilot is devastated, but the Little Prince explains that while his body will remain on Earth, his soul will return to his asteroid. The film ends with the pilot searching for the Little Prince in the desert, eventually giving up and flying away—but in the final scene, he hears the Little Prince's laughter in the stars.
entries.core:
Beneath its whimsical surface and musical numbers, The Little Prince is a profound meditation on adulthood, innocence, and what gives life meaning. The novella (and by extension this film) critiques how adults become absorbed in "serious matters"—power, wealth, status, accumulation—while losing touch with what truly matters: love, wonder, and human connection.

The Little Prince himself represents childhood innocence and wonder, asking questions that adults have stopped asking. His journey through the planets shows how adults become trapped by their own conceits—the King who rules nothing, the Vain man who no one admires, the Businessman who owns nothing but numbers, the Drunkard who is trapped in cycles of shame. Each planet represents a different adult trap.

The rose is perhaps the film's central symbol—something the Little Prince loves but doesn't fully understand. The rose's vulnerability (her four thorns, her need for protection) mirrors human relationships: we are responsible for what we tame, and that responsibility gives our lives meaning.

The film adaptation adds musical numbers that, while dated, provide additional emotional resonance. The themes of loneliness, love, loss, and the importance of maintaining wonder into adulthood remain powerful. Saint-Exupéry's message—that we must protect our inner child and remember that what matters most is invisible to the eyes—has only grown more relevant in an age of materialism and digital distraction.

The ending is deliberately ambiguous—is the Little Prince's body just a shell? Did he truly return to his asteroid? The film suggests that death is not an ending but a transformation, and that what we love never truly leaves us.

entries.title: The Long Dark
entries.creator: Hinterland Studio
entries.pitch:
The Long Dark is a survival video game set in the Canadian wilderness after a catastrophic geomagnetic storm knocks out global civilization. Players control Will Mackenzie, a bush pilot who crash-lands in the remote forests of northern Canada and must survive against the elements, wildlife, and the brutal cold. Unlike typical post-apocalyptic games focused on combat or zombies, The Long Dark emphasizes pure survival mechanics—managing body temperature, hunger, thirst, fatigue, and health while scavenging for supplies in a vast, unforgiving open world.

The game was developed by Hinterland Studio, a small Canadian developer founded by Raphael van Lierop after his work on Warhammer 40,000: Space Marine. Inspired by the Canadian wilderness of Vancouver Island, the team sought to create a post-apocalyptic game远离 urbanization tropes, instead exploring what survival looks like in nature's margins. The game was funded through Kickstarter in 2013 and released in Early Access in 2014 before a full release in 2017. It received critical acclaim for its survival mechanics and atmospheric design, selling over 750,000 copies by 2016.
entries.truth:
The Long Dark features two main game modes: Survival and Wintermute (story mode).

**Survival Mode** is the core experience—a permadeath open-world survival game where players must manage four core vitals: Body Temperature, Calories, Fatigue, and Hydration. Players scavenge for food, water, firewood, and supplies in a map spanning twelve major regions including Mystery Lake, Coastal Highway, Pleasant Valley, and Timberwolf Mountain. The environment is harsh: temperatures drop dangerously at night, blizzards reduce visibility to near zero, frostbite sets in quickly, and wildlife including wolves, bears, and moose pose constant threats.

Resources are finite—no respawning supplies means players must constantly move to new areas or carefully ration what they find. The game simulates realistic day/night cycles and weather systems that directly impact survival. Fire is essential for warmth and cooking, requiring players to balance gathering wood with other survival tasks. Different difficulty levels (Pilgrim, Voyageur, Stalker, Interloper, Misery) dramatically alter resource scarcity, wildlife aggression, and weather severity.

**Wintermute** is a narrative-driven episodic adventure following Will Mackenzie and his passenger Dr. Astrid Greenwood as they attempt to survive after the geomagnetic storm. The story mode guides players through various regions while introducing survival mechanics gradually, featuring voice acting and a more traditional adventure game structure with objectives and scripted events. The first two episodes were released at launch, with subsequent episodes added over time through 2021.

The game's story mode explores themes of isolation, human connection, and the fragility of modern civilization. The geomagnetic storm premise—while scientifically simplified—serves as a catalyst to examine what remains when technology fails and humans must rely on primal survival instincts.
entries.core:
**Nature as Antagonist:** Unlike most post-apocalyptic media where the primary threats are other humans, monsters, or societal collapse, The Long Dark makes nature the antagonist. The Canadian wilderness itself—the cold, the snow, the wildlife, the finite resources—is the enemy. This reframes the survival genre around human helplessness against natural forces rather than human-on-human conflict, creating a unique atmospheric tension rooted in environmental horror.

**Canadian Identity and Place:** The developers deliberately crafted a Canadian post-apocalypse, grounding the game in specific northern geography and culture. The regional place names, wildlife (moose, bears, timberwolves), and environmental conditions reflect the Canadian wilderness experience. Van Lierop explicitly stated a desire to explore post-apocalyptic fiction from the fringes rather than urban centers, embracing a Canadian identity distinct from American genre tropes.

**Permadeath and Consequence:** The survival mode's permadeath system creates genuine stakes for every decision. With no save-scumming and limited resources, each playthrough becomes a narrative of specific choices and consequences. This mechanical design reinforces the thematic content: in a world where everything is scarce, every action has weight.

**The Silence of Collapse:** The game's sound design emphasizes the silence of a depopulated world. Without the background hum of civilization, players experience profound isolation—the only sounds are wind, wildlife, and the player's own heartbeat. This auditory landscape reinforces themes of loneliness and the scale of what has been lost.

The Long Dark carved a unique niche in the survival game genre by rejecting conventional post-apocalyptic imagery (zombies, nuclear wasteland, urban decay) for a more naturalistic, atmospheric approach. Its success demonstrated appetite for survival games that prioritize environmental tension over combat, and helped establish the "cozy survival" subgenre while maintaining genuine difficulty and consequence.

entries.title: The Long Tomorrow
entries.creator: Dan O'Bannon (writer), Moebius (Jean Giraud) (illustrator)
entries.pitch: Set in a dystopian, crime-ridden future New York City in 2031, this graphic novel follows a cynical taxicab driver named Harry Canyon who navigates the mean streets in his disintegrator-equipped cab. When a red-haired woman fleeing gangsters seeks his help, Harry finds himself tangled in a deadly game involving the Loc-Nar—a green crystalline sphere that contains "the sum of all evils." Written by Dan O'Bannon and illustrated by Moebius, this was one of the first true cyberpunk stories, a pioneering work that influenced the visual designs of Blade Runner, Alien, Tron, and The Abyss. The story is a love letter to film noir and hardboiled crime fiction, transplanted into a bleak science fiction future.
entries.truth:
Harry Canyon is a world-weary taxi driver in New York City, 2031—a metropolis plagued by crime and corruption. Speaking in the classic hardboiled detective cadence, Harry narrates his daily grind: dodging fare-skippers and would-be robbers, using the disintegrator ray installed in his back seat to dispatch the occasional fool who tries to take him for a ride.

One evening, Harry stumbles into a violent confrontation between gangsters and a young red-haired woman. He rescues her from the gangster Rudnick, who murdered her father. She reveals that her father discovered an artifact called the Loc-Nar—a green, crystalline sphere that contains unimaginable power—and she's been hunted ever since by those who want it.

Harry takes her to his apartment, where they become intimate. She proposes a scheme: sell the Loc-Nar to Rudnick and split the money. At the exchange, when Rudnick attempts to take the artifact, the Loc-Nar disintegrates him. But the woman double-crosses Harry as well, trying to keep all the money for herself and pulling a gun. Harry is faster, using his disintegrator on her.

Harry keeps the money, sums up the adventure as "a two-day ride with one hell of a tip," and drives off into the neon-lit night—the Loc-Nar's corruptive influence claiming another victim, though one far from innocent.

The original comic was serialized in two segments in the French magazine Métal Hurlant in 1976, later reprinted in Moebius Book Four and published in America by Heavy Metal magazine. It was loosely adapted as the "Harry Canyon" segment of the 1981 animated anthology film Heavy Metal.
entries.core:
The Long Tomorrow is remarkable for being one of the first works to fully embody what would later be called cyberpunk—a term that wouldn't be coined until the early 1980s. Yet here, in 1975-76, O'Bannon and Moebius created the visual and thematic template: rain-soaked neon cities, technology as both tool and corruption, morally ambiguous protagonists navigating a future where the line between human and machine blurs.

The story is fundamentally about the corrupting nature of power. The Loc-Nar represents absolute evil—not as a metaphysical force, but as an amplifier of human greed and ambition. Everyone who encounters it, from the father who discovered it to the gangsters who pursue it to Harry himself, becomes corrupted. Even "winning" in this world means becoming complicit in violence.

Yet O'Bannon and Moebius tell this dark tale with a wry, satirical edge. The protagonist is a mercenary who doesn't care about justice—he just wants to get paid. The ending offers no redemption, no transformation—Harry is simply richer and slightly more dead inside. This cynical, noir-inflected view of the future was radical for its time and proved endlessly influential.

William Gibson himself acknowledged that the artwork in Heavy Metal (including The Long Tomorrow) shaped how Neuromancer "looked" on the page. The visual language of cyberpunk—mega-corporations, rain-slicked streets, holographic advertisements, noir anti-heroes—can trace its lineage directly to this unassuming graphic novel.

entries.title: The Long Way to a Small, Angry Planet
entries.creator: Becky Chambers
entries.pitch: When the crew of the aging spaceship Wayfarer takes on a dangerous job to punch a tunnel through space to reach a distant, volatile planet, they have no idea how this one journey will transform them all. The Long Way to a Small, Angry Planet is a character-driven space opera that prioritizes kindness, connection, and hope over traditional dramatic action. Follow the diverse crew—humans, aliens, and an artificial intelligence—as they navigate not just the vast distances between stars, but also their own personal histories, traumas, and the messy, beautiful work of building relationships across difference. This is science fiction as cozy catharsis, a story where the destination matters far less than the people you travel with.
entries.truth:
The novel centers on the crew of the Wayfarer, a small tunneling ship captained by the pragmatic and emotionally reserved Sissix. The crew is an unlikely family: Ohan, a member of the amorphous Aandrisk species who communicates through scent; Rosemary, a young woman running from her privileged past who signs on as the ship's cook; Jenks, a sentient artificial intelligence existing in a small portable unit; and several other colorful characters, each carrying their own burdens and secrets.

The story begins when the Wayfarer is hired by the sophisticated alien couple Ashby and Santedao (who go by "B迎" and "K Questions") to bore a tunnel through space to reach the distant planet Calledion-8, colloquially known as the "small, angry planet"—a world whose volatile nature has kept it isolated and mysterious. This dangerous job promises to pay enough to keep the Wayfarer running for years, but it will require traveling through unmapped space and boring through the naturally occurring tunnels that some species use for faster-than-light travel.

As the crew prepares for the journey, we learn their individual stories. Rosemary is hiding from her powerful family. Ohan is dealing with the death of their bondmate and questioning their role in Aandrisk society. Jenks, the AI, yearns for a body. Sissix carries guilt over a past failure. The engineer Dr. Chef processes his trauma as a former war prisoner. Each character gets space to exist as a full person, not just a function in the plot.

The journey itself becomes the story. The crew encounters various challenges—a run-in with pirates, a medical emergency, an ambush by a hostile alien species—but these external conflicts serve primarily as catalysts for character development rather than traditional action set pieces. The novel is famously low on conventional plot tension; instead, it builds slowly toward emotional revelations and moments of genuine connection.

The Calledion-8 portion of the journey becomes increasingly dangerous, and the crew must work together in ways they didn't expect. The planet itself proves challenging, but it's ultimately the relationships forged during the long journey that define the story. The book ends with the crew having completed their mission, fundamentally changed by their time together, having found something like family in the vastness of space.
entries.core:
The Long Way to a Small, Angry Planet represents what has come to be called "hopepunk" or "cozy sci-fi"—a deliberate counterpoint to the grimdark and cynical narratives that dominate science fiction. Chambers isn't interested in exploring humanity's worst impulses or the darkness of the universe; instead, she's concerned with how kindness, patience, and understanding can be radical acts of resistance.

The novel's central theme is connection across difference. Every character is alien in some way—literally or metaphorically—and the story argues that meaningful relationships require vulnerability, patience, and the willingness to be changed by others. Sissix's arc shows how emotional walls can isolate us; Rosemary's demonstrates how confronting your past is necessary for growth; Ohan's explores identity and belonging beyond one's species; Jenks's speaks to personhood and what it means to be alive.

Chambers also critiques extractive relationships and capitalistic exploitation through the contrast between the Wayfarer's crew and the corporate entities that dominate space. The crew operates on trust and shared purpose rather than profit motive—a quiet utopic vision that suggests another way of living together might be possible.

The title itself points to the book's philosophy: the journey matters more than the destination. The "small, angry planet" is almost anticlimactic; what truly matters is how the crew changed during the getting there. This is science fiction that suggests hope isn't naive—it's a choice, a practice, a form of courage in a universe that often seems indifferent to our wellbeing.

entries.title: The Longest Journey
entries.creator: Funcom / Ragnar Tørnquist
entries.pitch: The Longest Journey is a 1999 point-and-click adventure game developed by Norwegian studio Funcom, written by Ragnar Tørnquist. Set in the year 2209, it follows April Ryan, an art student in Newport who discovers she possesses the rare ability to shift between two parallel worlds: Stark, a science-driven future Earth, and Arcadia, a magical realm of dragons and ancient powers. The game was notable for blending science fiction and high fantasy elements in a single narrative, exploring themes of balance between technology and magic, reality and imagination. It received critical acclaim for its mature storytelling, complex protagonist, and ambitious world-building, selling over 500,000 copies by 2004 and spawning two sequels. The game is widely regarded as one of the greatest adventure games ever made.
entries.truth:
April Ryan is an art student living in Newport, a futuristic city on the U.S. West Coast in the year 2209. She has been experiencing strange dreams involving a white dragon and surreal visions that others dismiss as stress. After one particularly vivid dream where she meets the White Dragon, April finds herself experiencing reality shifts that blur the line between dream and waking life.

Seeking answers, April visits Manny Cortez, a mysterious local who reveals himself to be aware of the world's hidden truths. He sends April through a portal into Arcadia—a parallel world that exists alongside Earth, where magic reigns and fantastic creatures dwell. In Arcadia's city of Marcuria, April meets Tobias Grensret, a priest who explains the true history of the two worlds.

Thousands of years ago, Earth was divided into two parallel realms: Stark (science-based) and Arcadia (magic-based). This division was created by the Draic Kin—powerful dragon-like beings—to prevent humanity from abusing both science and magic. A Guardian was chosen to maintain balance between the worlds, living for a thousand years to protect "The Balance."

April discovers she is a Shifter, someone who can move freely between Stark and Arcadia. She learns that the current Guardian has abandoned their post, throwing the Balance into chaos. A faction called the Vanguard in Stark seeks to reunite the worlds prematurely, which would cause catastrophe.

Throughout her journey, April must collect ancient artifacts called Draic Kin gems, navigate political intrigue in both worlds, and ultimately decide whether to accept her role as a potential successor to the Guardian. She is aided by Crow, a talking bird who becomes her companion. The story explores the consequences of forcing unity between two worlds that have evolved separately—one path leads to destruction, the other to a fragile peace.
entries.core:
**The Balance Between Opposites:** The Longest Journey's central theme is the need for balance between opposing forces—science and magic, logic and emotion, order and chaos. The game presents these not as good versus evil, but as complementary forces that must coexist in harmony. The Guardians represent this balance, living for centuries to maintain equilibrium between worlds that would destroy each other if reunited prematurely. This theme resonates with broader philosophical questions about dualism and the dangers of extremes.

**Identity and Transformation:** April Ryan undergoes profound transformation throughout the game, discovering abilities she never knew she had and confronting a destiny she never asked for. Her journey explores questions of self-discovery, personal growth, and accepting responsibility for something larger than oneself. As a Shifter, she literally embodies transformation—moving between identities and worlds. Her character arc represents the universal struggle to define oneself amid unexpected circumstances.

**The Cost of Progress:** The game contrasts Stark's technological advancement with Arcadia's magical wisdom, questioning whether progress in one direction necessarily means loss in another. The Vanguard represents those who believe merging the worlds would solve all problems, but the game suggests such forced unity would be catastrophic. This commentary on unexamined technological optimism echoes real-world debates about unchecked advancement.

**Female Protagonism in Games:** April Ryan stands as one of gaming's earliest complex female protagonists. Rather than being sexualized or reduced to a love interest, she is portrayed as intelligent, flawed, and determined—a fully realized character whose agency drives the entire narrative. This was notable in 1999 and remains significant, influencing how female characters would be written in subsequent adventure games.

The Longest Journey endures because it tackles big questions through personal storytelling—asking what happens when worlds collide, when identity becomes fluid, and when the choice between opposing forces isn't clear-cut. Its blend of sci-fi and fantasy was unusual for its time, and its mature themes set a standard for narrative-driven games that followed.

entries.title: The Lost City
entries.pitch: The Lost City is a 12-chapter science fiction serial from 1935, following scientist Bruce Gordon who travels to a remote region of Africa after tracing a series of mysterious electrical natural disasters to their source. There he discovers the hidden kingdom of the Lemurians, ruled by the mad scientist Zolok, the last survivor of the ancient civilization. Zolok has been using his scientific knowledge to create devastating disasters around the world as part of his scheme to take over the world, while holding the brilliant Dr. Manyus and his daughter Natcha hostage in his mountain complex. Gordon must help the hostages escape and stop Zolok's plans for global domination, encountering jungle adventures, slave traders, and a native queen along the way.
entries.truth:
The serial begins with scientist Bruce Gordon investigating a series of catastrophic electrical natural disasters that have been striking various locations worldwide. His research leads him to conclude that these events originate from a specific region in Africa. Upon arrival, Gordon discovers a hidden civilization existing within a mountain complex—the last remaining outpost of the Lemurians, an ancient race.

The mad scientist Zolok, who claims to be the last of the Lemurians, has been using his advanced scientific knowledge to orchestrate the global disasters as a prelude to his plan for world domination. Zolok has been holding Dr. Manyus, a brilliant scientist, and his daughter Natcha captive in his mountain lair, forcing Manyus to create an army of mindless giant slaves from the local natives. Additionally, Manyus has transformed another tribe, the spider-worshipping Wangas, into thin, impotent beings under Zolok's control.

Gordon helps Dr. Manyus and Natcha escape from Zolok's clutches, but their escape leads them into further danger. They encounter Ben Ali, a malignant slave trader, and meet Queen Rama, a helpful native queen who assists them in their quest. The group must survive harrowing jungle adventures while evading Zolok's forces before finally returning to the Lost City to confront the mad scientist and prevent his scheme from coming to fruition.

The serial consists of twelve chapters: "Living Dead-Men," "The Tunnel of Flame," "Dagger Rock," "Doomed," "Tiger Prey," "Human Beasts," "Spider Men," "Human Targets," "Jungle Vengeance," "The Lion Pit," "Death Ray," and "The Mad Scientist."
entries.core:
**Lost Civilization Tropes:** The Lost City represents a classic example of 1930s pulp science fiction's fascination with lost civilizations and hidden kingdoms. The Lemurians, referencing the mythical lost continent of Lemuria, provide an exotic setting that combines adventure serial conventions with science fiction elements. This trope would continue in serials and films for decades to come.

**Mad Scientist Genre:** Zolok embodies the archetypal mad scientist threatening world domination—a character type that was extremely popular in 1930s horror and science fiction films. His plan to use scientific knowledge for global conquest, complete with death rays and mind-controlled armies, reflects contemporary anxieties about the potential misuse of technology.

**Adventure Serial Structure:** The film follows the typical chapter play format, with each episode ending in a cliffhanger that would bring audiences back the following week. The combination of jungle adventure, scientific fantasy, and serial storytelling was a winning formula that drew audiences to movie palaces across America.

**Pre-Code Sensibility:** Released in 1935, The Lost City reflects the pre-Code Hollywood era's willingness to include more mature themes, including references to slavery, exotic cultures, and scientific experiments that might have been toned down in later years.

The serial was independently produced and later edited into four different feature versions, possibly a record for a single serial. It influenced later productions combining science fiction with lost world adventure themes.

entries.title: The Lost World
entries.creator: Harry O. Hoyt (Director)
entries.pitch: The Lost World is a 1925 American silent adventure film that holds a special place in cinema history as one of the first adaptations of Arthur Conan Doyle's 1912 novel and a landmark in special effects. Directed by Harry O. Hoyt and pioneering stop-motion animator Willis O'Brien (who would later create King Kong), this film brings to life the story of Professor Challenger and his expedition to a hidden plateau in South America where dinosaurs still roam. The film was a major commercial success upon release and was selected for preservation in the United States National Film Registry in 1998 for its cultural, historical, and aesthetic significance.
entries.truth:
The film adapts Arthur Conan Doyle's novel about an expedition to a remote plateau in South America where prehistoric creatures have survived. The story begins when Paula White arrives at Professor Challenger's London home with the journal of her father, explorer Maple White, containing sketches of dinosaurs found on a remote plateau. Despite academic ridicule, Challenger convinces a newspaper to fund an expedition to prove dinosaurs still exist.

The expedition team includes Professor Challenger, Paula White, sportsman Sir John Roxton, reporter Edward Malone (who hopes the expedition will impress his fiancée), skeptical Professor Summerlee, and Challenger's butler Austin, along with servant Zambo. Upon reaching the base of the plateau, the explorers face immediate danger when attacked by an Apeman. The conflict escalates when they discover living dinosaurs, including a Pteranodon—proof that Maple White's claims were true.

The team crosses onto the plateau using a fallen tree as a bridge, encountering various prehistoric creatures. The most memorable sequence involves a climactic battle between a Tyrannosaurus rex and a Triceratops, showcased through O'Brien's groundbreaking stop-motion animation. The film reaches its dramatic conclusion as the explorers must find a way to escape the plateau with their discoveries.
entries.core:
**Pioneering Special Effects:** The Lost World is historically significant for featuring some of the most impressive stop-motion animation of its era. Willis O'Brien's work on the film established him as a master of the technique and directly led to his employment on King Kong (1933). The dinosaur sequences, though primitive by modern standards, were revolutionary for 1925 and influenced decades of creature features to follow.

**Victorian Adventure Narrative:** The film embodies the early 20th-century adventure genre, drawing from Conan Doyle's literary tradition of intrepid explorers and mysterious lands. It reflects the era's optimism about exploration and scientific discovery, while also tapping into enduring fascinations with lost worlds and living fossils.

**Adaptation and Literary Heritage:** As one of the earliest filmed adaptations of The Lost World novel, the film helped establish the template for future dinosaur media. The story predates Jurassic Park by decades and helped cement the dinosaur adventure narrative in popular culture.

The Lost World remains significant as a historical artifact that demonstrates the early intersection of science fiction literature and cinema, showcasing how filmmakers began translating speculative fiction into visual storytelling. Its selection for the National Film Registry recognizes its lasting contribution to American cinema and the evolution of special effects in filmmaking.

entries.title: The Lunar Trilogy
entries.creator: Jerzy Żuławski
entries.pitch: The Lunar Trilogy is a groundbreaking Polish science fiction work written between 1901 and 1911, consisting of three novels: "On the Silver Globe" (1903), "The Conqueror" (1910), and "The Old Earth" (1911). Written by Jerzy Żuławski, one of Poland's most influential early science fiction authors, this trilogy tells the epic story of Earth astronauts stranded on the Moon who found a colony that, over centuries, develops its own civilization, religion, and destiny. The first volume is presented as a diary recovered from the Moon, describing the original expedition's tragic landing and the founding of the Selenite colony. The trilogy is considered one of the earliest works of modern science fiction and has been translated into Russian, Czech, German, English, and Hungarian.
entries.truth:
The first volume, "On the Silver Globe" (Na Srebrnym Globie), begins with an introduction set 50 years after a lunar expedition vanished. The core narrative is the diary of Jan Korecki, one of five astronauts who crash-land on the Moon. After the deaths of the expedition leader O'Tamor and the English physician Tomasz Woodbell, only Korecki, the Polish narrator, the Brazilian engineer Piotr Varadol, and Marta (a secret substitute for a German participant) survive. After several generations of inbreeding, their descendants lose most knowledge of Earth and form the Selenite civilization, ruled by a religious cult awaiting a savior from Earth.

The second volume, "The Conqueror" (Zwycięzca), takes place centuries later when another astronaut, Marek, arrives on the Moon. The Selenites, oppressed by the telepathic, devil-like Szerns who rule them, hail Marek as their long-awaited messiah. After leading a successful revolution against the Szerns, Marek attempts to reform Selenite society along socialist lines but fails when the population turns against him. He is ultimately martyred in a manner echoing the crucifixion of Christ.

The third volume, "The Old Earth" (Stara Ziemia), follows two Selenites who travel to 27th-century Earth, discovering a humanity that has advanced technologically but faces its own crises. The trilogy explores themes of religion, messianism, revolution, and the cyclical nature of civilization.
entries.core:
The Lunar Trilogy is remarkable for its early treatment of themes that would become central to science fiction: space exploration, alien civilizations, dystopia, and the religious and social implications of contact between cultures. Żuławski's work predates many classic science fiction works and demonstrates remarkable sophistication in its treatment of colonization, cultural degeneration, and messianic narratives.

The trilogy influenced later Polish science fiction and established many themes that would be explored by successors like Stanisław Lem. Its treatment of a society losing its original culture and developing new religious traditions, its critique of revolutionary movements, and its philosophical exploration of human nature and destiny make it a foundational work of Eastern European science fiction.

The third volume's critique of 27th-century Earth as a society that has achieved technological progress but lost its spiritual and moral foundation remains relevant particularly, anticipating later dystopian works by over a decade. The trilogy's influence extends beyond literature—it was adapted into a film by Andrzej Żuławski, the author's grandson, though the film project was famously never completed.

entries.title: The Machine Stops
entries.creator: E. M. Forster
entries.pitch:
In a distant future, humanity has retreated underground to escape a toxic, uninhabitable Earth surface. Every person lives in isolation within a single room, their every need met by the all-powerful global Machine—from food and air to entertainment and communication. Physical travel is rare; instead, people connect through instantaneous video-like devices, sharing endless secondhand ideas without ever truly experiencing life firsthand.

When Vashti, content in her isolated existence, is summoned by her rebellious son Kuno, she embarks on a rare journey across the world to meet him. Kuno has discovered something that threatens everything their society believes—he has visited the Earth's surface and found humans living freely outside the Machine's control. His revelations about the Machine's true nature and his warning of impending collapse fall on deaf ears. This haunting early sci-fi tale explores humanity's dangerous dependence on technology and the spiritual cost of living disconnected from nature and each other.
entries.truth:
The story takes place in a post-apocalyptic future where the Earth's surface has become uninhabitable due to extreme climate change and toxic air. Humanity survives in vast underground colonies, each person confined to a standardized room within the Machine—a colossal, self-regulating system that provides absolutely everything needed for survival. Humans have lost the ability to live naturally; they are fed, sheltered, entertained, and connected solely through the Machine's infrastructure.

The two central characters are Vashti and her son Kuno, who live on opposite sides of the world. Vashti represents the ideal citizen of this mechanical civilization—she spends her days producing and discussing secondhand ideas, content to remain in her room where all her needs are satisfied. She uses her work as a shield against genuine human connection, preferring the sanitized interaction her world offers. Travel to the surface exists but is discouraged and rarely undertaken, considered uncomfortable and unnecessary.

Kuno, however, is different. He is passionate, free-spirited, and increasingly troubled by the artificiality of their existence. He manages to obtain a respirator and secretly strengthens his body enough to journey to the Earth's surface, where he discovers others living freely outside the Machine's embrace. When the Machine discovers his transgression, he is threatened with "Homelessness"—expulsion from the underground world and its life-support systems, which amounts to a death sentence.

Kuno persuades Vashti to make the difficult journey to his room. He shares his experiences and his growing conviction that the Machine is not the benevolent provider they believe it to be. Vashti listens but cannot accept his perspective, viewing his rebellion as madness. She returns to her part of the world, dismissing his concerns.

Time passes, and two significant developments occur. First, after Kuno's escape, respirators are banned—most citizens welcome this, fearful of direct experience. Second, a religion called "Mechanism" emerges, worshipping the Machine as a divine entity. People forget that humans created the Machine and now treat it as a mystical force whose needs supersede their own. Those who question this faith face Homelessness.

Kuno is eventually relocated near Vashti. He becomes convinced the Machine is failing and warns her cryptically: "The Machine stops." At first, they dismiss minor malfunctions as the Machine's whim. But defects multiply, and it becomes clear that the knowledge of how to repair the Machine has been lost entirely.

The Machine finally collapses, taking down the entire underground civilization with it. In the chaos, Kuno reaches Vashti's failing room—where air, water, and food have stopped. For the first time since his childhood, they physically embrace. As they face death together, they recognize that genuine human connection and harmony with nature matter above all else. The story ends with the suggestion that surface-dwellers still exist and may rebuild humanity without repeating the Machine's mistake.
entries.core:
E.M. Forster's 1909 masterpiece delivers a searing critique of technological dependency that feels remarkably prescient over a century later. The story warns against surrendering human agency to the very systems we create—suggesting that when technology becomes an end rather than a means, humanity loses its essential self.

The central theme explores what Forster calls "the sin against the body"—the danger of intellectual and spiritual refinement that severs us from our physical existence and adaptive capabilities. The underground citizens have become so refined that they cannot survive in natural environments; they have traded authentic experience for comfortable illusion.

The Machine Stops also examines spiritual emptiness in a material world. The emergence of Mechanism—religion worshipping the created rather than the creator—represents humanity's tendency to deify its own creations and surrender autonomy to them. The citizens don't serve the Machine; they serve what they believe the Machine wants, having forgotten their own power.

Perhaps most striking is Forster's prediction of technologies like the internet, video conferencing, and global connectivity—imagined as tools that would paradoxically increase human isolation. The story asks whether virtual connection can ever substitute for physical presence and direct experience.

This work influenced countless sci-fi works, from THX 1138 to Logan's Run to Neon Genesis Evangelion. Its warnings about technological dependency, isolation, and the loss of bodily autonomy resonate more urgently today than ever before.

entries.title: The Man Who Came Early
entries.pitch: What if a modern soldier suddenly found himself in Viking-era Iceland? That's the premise of this delightful comic gem from Poul Anderson. Our protagonist is a 20th-century American soldier who, through some unexplained temporal anomaly, is transported back to the 10th century just as the Norse sagas were being written. The story follows his misadventures as he tries to navigate a world of longships, blood feuds, and gods he doesn't believe in—while the locals think he's either mad or a sorcerer. It's a witty, playful exploration of culture clash and the absurdity of appearing "advanced" in a society that has its own sophisticated traditions. The humor comes from the collision between modern assumptions and medieval practicality.
entries.truth:
The story opens with a US Army soldier stationed in post-WWII Germany who suddenly finds himself transported through time. He awakens in 10th-century Iceland, naked and confused, in what turns out to be the era when the sagas were being composed. 

The locals initially think he's a madman or perhaps a ghost. He's taken in by a farming family who give him clothes and food, though they regard him with suspicion. The soldier, convinced he can "improve" their lives with modern knowledge, makes a series of hilariously misguided attempts to help. He tries to introduce better farming techniques, explain germ theory, and demonstrate "superior" military tactics—only to discover that these medieval Icelanders aren't the ignorant savages he assumed.

Through a series of comedic encounters, he learns that these people have their own sophisticated culture, legal system (the Althing), and way of life. The climax comes when he attempts to prevent a local blood feud by explaining modern conflict resolution—only to find that the Icelanders have a more elegant system already. By the end, the soldier realizes he's the one who has been educated, and he becomes something of a wise fool in this society, his strange prophecies and anachronistic knowledge adding to his mystique.

The story ends with him settling into this new world, his modern arrogance beaten down, gaining a kind of humble wisdom. Whether he ever returns to his own time remains ambiguous—the implication is he's found a better life among people he once looked down upon.
entries.core:
This story is a pointed satire on technological and cultural arrogance. Anderson uses the time-travel premise to deconstruct the idea that "progress" is linear and that modern people are inherently superior to those who came before us. The soldier's attempts to bring "civilization" to Viking Iceland comically backfire because he fails to recognize that these people have their own complex civilization—one that might actually handle certain problems better than the 20th century does.

The themes include:
- **Cultural humility**: The story argues that dismissing historical societies as primitive is itself a form of ignorance
- **The illusion of progress**: Not all "advances" are improvements; some things were done better in the past
- **Wisdom vs. knowledge**: The soldier has information but lacks wisdom; the Icelanders have wisdom but different information

What's fascinating from a storytelling perspective is how Anderson makes the medieval Icelanders fully realized characters with their own logic, humor, and depth. The soldier is the outsider, the one who must learn—and that's a refreshing inversion of the typical sci-fi "enlightening the primitives" narrative. It's a reminder that intelligence and sophistication exist in every age, and that learning to listen might be the most advanced thing anyone can do.

entries.title: The Man Who Fell to Earth
entries.creator: Directed by Nicolas Roeg; Based on the novel by Walter Tevis
entries.pitch: Thomas Jerome Newton is an extraterrestrial who crash-lands on Earth in New Mexico, appearing as a humanoid Englishman. He comes from a distant planet suffering from catastrophic drought, and his mission is to find a way to ship water back home. Using advanced technology from his world, Newton patents countless inventions and amasses tremendous wealth as the head of a technology conglomerate. But as he becomes entangled in human society—forming a relationship with a lonely hotel worker named Mary-Lou—he finds himself trapped by human vices, corruption, and his own growing addiction to alcohol and television. David Bowie delivers a mesmerizing, alien performance in this surreal meditation on isolation, capitalism, and the impossibility of going home.
entries.truth:
Thomas Jerome Newton arrives on Earth from a dying planet, using his advanced technology to quickly acquire wealth and patents as the head of World Enterprises Corporation. His goal is to finance a spaceship capable of carrying water back to his homeworld. While in New Mexico, he meets Mary-Lou, a young woman from Oklahoma working part-time jobs at a hotel. She introduces him to human customs—church, alcohol, sex—and they move in together near where he first landed.

Dr. Nathan Bryce, a former professor who had affairs with his students, becomes Newton's confidant as a fuel technician at World Enterprises. Bryce suspects Newton's alien nature and secretly photographs him with a special X-ray camera, revealing his extraterrestrial physiology. When Newton discovers Bryce knows his secret, he reveals his true form to Mary-Lou, who flees in horror.

Newton completes his spaceship for its maiden voyage, but just before takeoff, he is seized by the government and a rival company. His business partner Farnsworth is murdered. Newton is held captive in a luxury apartment within a hotel, sedated with alcohol and subjected to medical tests. Years pass. Mary-Lou returns, now older and ravaged by alcohol. They spend their time drinking and playing table tennis. She declares she no longer loves him; he agrees.

Eventually Newton escapes his derelict prison, but he cannot return home. A despondent, alcoholic Newton creates a radio recording with alien messages intended for his home planet. Bryce, now married to Mary-Lou, buys a copy of the album and meets Newton at a restaurant. Newton remains young and wealthy, but has fallen deeply into depression and alcoholism. He asks about Mary-Lou before collapsing in a drunken stupor—forever changed, forever trapped on Earth.
entries.core:
Nicolas Roeg's film is a haunting exploration of alienation and the corrupting nature of human systems. Newton comes as a savior to his dying world, armed with technology far beyond human understanding, yet he is destroyed not by any external threat but by human vice itself. The film critiques capitalism through Newton's transformation from noble savior to corrupted corporate mogul—his mission to save his planet becomes subverted by the very systems of wealth and power he must exploit to achieve it.

The film also explores the impossibility of true connection across difference. Despite forming genuine relationships with Mary-Lou and Bryce, Newton remains fundamentally alone, his alien nature making authentic human connection impossible. His addiction to alcohol and television—consuming multiple screens at once—represents both his alienation and his slow assimilation into human decay.

Visually, Roeg's surreal, non-linear editing creates a disorienting experience that mirrors Newton's fractured perception. The film raises profound questions about what it means to be human, what we sacrifice in pursuit of progress, and whether salvation is possible when one's mission conflicts with the corrupt systems required to accomplish it.

entries.title: The Man Who Haunted Himself
entries.creator: Directed by Basil Dearden; Based on the novel "The Strange Case of Mr Pelham" by Anthony Armstrong
entries.pitch: When business executive Harold Pelham survives a near-fatal car crash, he returns home to find that people have been seeing him in places he doesn't remember being—behaving in ways completely out of character. His wife, colleagues, and friends all claim to have interacted with him at times when he was supposedly elsewhere. As Harold investigates, he becomes increasingly paranoid, convinced that a doppelganger is living his life. But the horrifying truth may be far stranger than any conspiracy: Harold may not be the original at all.
entries.truth:
Harold Pelham is a successful director at Freeman, Pelham & Dawson, a marine technology company. One day, while driving home from his London office, he suddenly imagines himself in a different car and crashes horrifically. On the operating table, he briefly dies, and the monitor shows two heartbeats before he is revived.

After recovering, Harold notices odd things happening—people act strangely around him, and he finds himself accused of behavior he can't recall. His wife mentions a mysterious silver car parked outside their home. At work, he's been secretly supporting a merger he now opposes. Friends and colleagues claim they've seen him at places he never visited, doing things he has no memory of.

Harold confronts an executive from the rival company, who reminds him of three secret meetings they supposedly had—including at the London Planetarium and in a boat on The Serpentine. Someone is masquerading as him, conducting clandestine affairs and business dealings.

Desperate, Harold consults psychiatrist Dr. Harris and undergoes treatment. During his time in the clinic, the double finalizes the merger and goes on a date with his wife, culminating in them having sex together—the real Harold has been replaced in every way that matters.

When Harold calls home and hears "himself" answer the phone, he races home and confronts his double. Both insist they are the real Pelham. Their high-speed chase ends on a bridge when Harold swerves into the river. Just before impact, his image fades away.

The double survives, experiencing a brief moment of pain (the sound of a double heartbeat), then returns to calm: there is once more only one Pelham.
entries.core:
The Man Who Haunted Himself is a clever variation on the classic doppelganger theme, updating the Jekyll and Hyde formula for a 1970s corporate world. The film explores themes of identity, authenticity, and the existential terror of being replaced—of wondering whether the self is truly continuous or can be duplicated, overwritten, or supplanted.

Thecorporate setting is crucial: the film suggests that identity itself has become commodified. The double isn't a supernatural evil; he's simply a more ruthless, opportunistic version of Harold who knows how to play the game of business and relationships. In a sense, the film asks whether modern professional life has already created multiple selves—which one is "real"?

The near-death experience is the mechanism that "releases" the double, suggesting that identity is fragile and contingent—something that can be split and copied. The final twist, where the original dissolves while the double survives, implies that the "self" is ultimately an illusion, and the more adaptable version wins.

The film's quiet horror lies in its intimate scale: this isn't a world-threatening conspiracy, just one man's existential crisis. By grounding the supernatural in the mundane realities of marriage and office politics, the film suggests that the horror of identity loss is always personal.

entries.title: The Man Who Lost the Sea
entries.creator: Theodore Sturgeon
entries.pitch: "The Man Who Lost the Sea" is a haunting meditation on memory, grief, and what it means to be human, told through the fractured consciousness of an astronaut recovering from serious injuries. As the protagonist drifts between waking life and feverish memory, we experience fragments of his past: his training as an astronaut, his loving relationship with his wife, and the tragedy that has left him broken. Sturgeon weaves these temporal threads together in a narrative structure that mimics how the human mind actually processes trauma—non-linearly, emotionally, and with painful clarity. This is science fiction at its most literary: less interested in technology or space exploration than in the interior landscape of a single human heart confronting loss.
entries.truth:
The story opens with the protagonist in a hospital bed, his body broken from some unspecified accident, his mind floating between consciousness and memory. He is an astronaut—part of a program that has sent men into space—but he is now earthbound, possibly permanently. The circumstances of his injuries remain模糊 (his wife has died, that's clear, but the exact mechanism of his current condition is deliberately obscured).

We learn through fragmented memories that he had a wife named Kathryn (or something similar), a woman who understood him completely and who loved him despite—or perhaps because of—his obsession with space. Their relationship was tender, humorous, and deeply intimate. We see moments: her laughter at his jokes, their discussions about what lies beyond the stars, the ordinary miracle of shared meals and quiet evenings.

But there is tragedy here. Something happened. His wife is dead, and the protagonist is haunted not just by her absence but by guilt. Did his work take him away from her too often? Did his obsession with the stars cost him the chance to be fully present in their life together? The story doesn't provide easy answers; it simply sits with the pain.

As the protagonist's memories progress, we see his training as an astronaut—the rigorous physical preparation, the psychological screening, the moment when he first saw Earth from space and understood, perhaps for the first time, both the vastness of the universe and the precious fragility of human life. We see his relationship with his fellow astronauts, men who have glimpsed the impossible and returned changed.

The present-tense scenes in the hospital grow increasingly poignant as the protagonist struggles to reconcile his past self—the ambitious explorer, the hopeful husband—with who he is now: a broken man who may never fly again, who has lost his wife, who is facing a future he never planned for. The story ends ambiguously: we are never certain if he will recover, if he will find peace, if he will fly again. What we have is the present moment, tender and terrible, and the accumulated weight of a life lived in the shadow of the infinite.
entries.core:
At its heart, "The Man Who Lost the Sea" is about the cost of aspiration. The protagonist has dedicated his life to reaching the stars, to pushing the boundaries of human experience—and that dedication has cost him. His wife is gone. His body is broken. And yet, the story suggests, the aspiration itself was not wrong; it was simply part of being fully human. We are creatures who dream of flying, who look up at the night sky and feel the pull of the unknown. To be fully alive is to want things that might destroy us.

Sturgeon also explores memory as a construct that shapes identity. The protagonist's memories are not accurate recordings; they are colored by emotion, distorted by time, selected by a mind trying to make sense of pain. The story suggests that we are, in some sense, the stories we tell ourselves about our past—and those stories can heal or harm us.

Finally, the story meditates on loneliness and connection. The protagonist has lost his wife, but he is not entirely alone—he has his fellow astronauts, his handlers, the medical staff. Yet his deepest connection was with Kathryn, and without her, he exists in a kind of isolation that no amount of human contact can fully penetrate. The story asks whether it's better to have loved and lost than never to have loved at all, and its answer is complex: yes, obviously—but the loss doesn't stop hurting, and the love doesn't stop mattering, and both facts coexist without resolution.

entries.title: The Man Who Never Grew Old
entries.creator: C.M. Kornbluth
entries.pitch: In a future where humanity has conquered aging and extended lifespans to approximately 200 years, one man exists outside this biological revolution. He does not age. While his friends grow old and die around him—generation after generation—he remains forever young. This haunting short story explores the profound loneliness of immortality through the eyes of a man who has watched everyone he ever loved turn to dust. Published in the December 1952 issue of If magazine, this is one of science fiction's most poignant meditations on mortality, memory, and what it means to be human in a world where time has lost its meaning for everyone except one.
entries.truth:
The story centers on John H. Wilson, a man who has lived for over 300 years without aging a single day. In this future world, medical science has conquered senescence—humans now routinely live to 200, some even to 250. But Wilson is different. He stopped aging sometime in his thirties and has remained there for centuries.

The narrative unfolds through Wilson's interactions with his latest circle of friends, particularly a woman named Elaine and her husband Robert. As Wilson watches them age—from middle age into their final years—he reflects on the endless cycle of forming attachments and losing them. The story is not about dramatic action; it's about the weight of accumulated memory and the impossibility of connection when you know you'll outlive everyone.

Wilson's situation becomes increasingly precarious as the government takes notice. The authorities are baffled by his apparent immunity to their age-retarding treatments. They see him as either a medical miracle or a threat to the established order. The story culminates in Wilson's decision to disappear—to leave behind the world where he has become too conspicuous and start again somewhere new, knowing he will eventually face the same isolation again.

The ending is bittersweet: Wilson understands that his condition is both a gift and a curse. He will never experience death, but he will never be free of grief. The final lines suggest he's learned to find meaning in the brief connections he makes, knowing they're all he'll ever have.
entries.core:
At its heart, "The Man Who Never Grew Old" is about the unbearable loneliness of immortality. Kornbluth, known for his satirical edge, here turns to something more contemplative—exploring what happens to the human spirit when it must watch generations of loved ones age and die while remaining frozen in time.

The story predates many of science fiction's famous immortality tales and offers a more grounded, psychological approach. Rather than treating eternal youth as a superpower, Kornbluth presents it as a curse—one that isolates the protagonist from the very humanity he seeks to preserve.

The theme of memory is also central. Wilson carries centuries of memories, relationships, and losses. The story asks whether such accumulated weight is a form of wisdom or a form of madness. Can anyone truly process the death of everyone they've ever loved?

Finally, there's a subtle critique of transhumanist aspirations. The future world in the story has "conquered" aging, but this hasn't brought utopia—only new social problems and the realization that mortality gives life its meaning. Wilson's eternal youth becomes a mirror showing humanity what it might lose in its quest to defeat death.

This is science fiction at its most literary—less interested in technology and speculation than in the interiority of its characters and the timeless questions of existence.

entries.title: The Man from Beyond
entries.creator: Burton L. King
entries.pitch: The Man from Beyond is a 1922 American silent mystery film starring Harry Houdini as Howard Hillary, a man found frozen in arctic ice who is brought back to life by a team of explorers. When Howard revives, scientist Dr. Gregory Sinclair decides not to tell him that he is 100 years behind the times, planning to study his reactions as he readjusts to civilization. The film takes an intriguing turn when Howard discovers that his lost love Felice has been reincarnated as a young woman in the present day—creating a poignant tale of love that transcends death and time. Houdini wrote the story himself, and the film represents his attempt to reconcile with his friend Arthur Conan Doyle over their differing views on spiritualism—Doyle was a fervent advocate of the supernatural while Houdini was its most famous critic.
entries.truth:
The film opens with Dr. Gregory Sinclair and his fellow explorers discovering a man frozen solid in arctic ice. They chop him out and thaw him, bringing Howard Hillary back to life. Howard believes he is still in 1820, where he was a sailor on a ship that suffered a mutiny. He remembers trying to save the captain—Felice's father—when he received a blow to the head and froze.

Dr. Sinclair doesn't tell Howard the truth about the century that has passed. Instead, he brings Howard back to civilization to study his reactions. When they return to Sinclair's home, Howard learns that his ward's father, Dr. Crawford Strange, had set out to join the arctic expedition but was lost. It is the wedding day of Sinclair's ward, Felice Strange.

When Howard sees Felice, he is stunned—he calls out to her, begging her to recall their love from his past life. Felice shares Howard's memories through some supernatural connection; she is the reincarnation of his lost sweetheart from 1820. The wedding is postponed as Felice begins to remember her past life with Howard.

Meanwhile, Dr. Gilbert Trent, a discarded suitor, has kidnapped Felice's father and is holding him in the cellar of his house. He plans to marry Felice and take her to the arctic. Howard and Sinclair discover a clue—a hat with Dr. Strange's initials—and rescue him. Felice is pursued by Trent's henchman, and Howard follows them to the edge of Niagara Falls. Howard swims the rapids, rescues Felice, and confronts Trent on a cliff where Trent falls to his death. Felice recognizes Howard as her true mate across time.

The film ends with the words "You must believe"—a nod to Doyle's spiritualist beliefs.
entries.core:
**Reincarnation and Memory:** The Man from Beyond explores the concept of reincarnation, with Felice being the reincarnation of Howard's lost love from 1820. The film suggests that love and memory can transcend death and time, connecting souls across generations.

**Houdini and Spiritualism:** The film is notable in the context of Houdini's public disagreement with Arthur Conan Doyle over spiritualism. While Doyle believed deeply in the supernatural, Houdini made his career exposing fraudulent mediums. The Man from Beyond represents Houdini's attempt to find common ground—he endorsed reincarnation as a concept while maintaining his skepticism about mediums claiming to contact the dead. Doyle praised the film, calling it "one of the really great contributions to the screen."

**Early Sci-Fi Themes:** The film touches on cryonics and suspended animation—Howard is frozen for 100 years and revived, a concept that was groundbreaking in 1922 and remains relevant today. The "frozen man revived" premise is one of the earliest examples of cryonics in popular fiction.

**Silent Era Spectacle:** The film delivers visual spectacle, including the opening scene of chopping a frozen man from ice and the dramatic Niagara Falls rescue sequence. Houdini performed all his own stunts, adding authenticity to the action sequences.

entries.title: The Man from Earth
entries.creator: Richard Schenkman (director), Jerome Bixby (writer)
entries.pitch: The Man from Earth is a 2007 American science fiction drama film directed by Richard Schenkman and written by Jerome Bixby. The entire film takes place in real-time during a farewell party, as professor John Oldman reveals to his colleagues that he is more than 14,000 years old. Originally conceived in the early 1960s, Bixby completed the screenplay on his deathbed in April 1998. Shot on a budget of just $200,000 in eight days, the film gained significant recognition through internet peer-to-peer distribution, exceeding the filmmakers' expectations for visibility.
entries.truth:
Professor John Oldman is packing his belongings into his truck, preparing to move away from the university where he has taught. His colleagues—Harry the biologist, Edith the art historian, Dan the anthropologist, Sandy the historian, Art the archaeologist, and student Linda—arrive for an impromptu farewell party. As they pressure him to explain his sudden departure, John reveals that he was born in the Paleolithic period and has lived for over 14,000 years, relocating every ten years to avoid detection of his lack of aging.

John claims he was a Sumerian for 2000 years, later a Babylonian, and eventually a disciple of the Buddha. He also suggests he may have been the inspiration for the Jesus story. His colleagues, each from different academic backgrounds, question his claims based on their expertise. Art is the most skeptical, while others consider the possibility his story might be true.

Dr. Will Gruber, a psychiatry professor summoned by Art, arrives and questions John's mental state, even threatening him with an unloaded gun. Will later learns that John's story might be legitimate when he realizes one of John's past aliases was his father's name. Before John can fully explain, Will suffers a fatal heart attack. The film ends ambiguously—John leaves with Sandy, suggesting she believes his story.
entries.core:
**Immortality and Isolation:** The central theme explores the psychological burden of living forever, watching everyone you love grow old and die. John's repeated statement that he "just keeps moving" speaks to the loneliness of immortality—never settling, never allowing deep connections that would inevitably end in loss.

**Faith vs. Reason:** The film poses uncomfortable questions about religion, suggesting that a mortal man could have inspired religious figures. This challenges both religious literalism (represented by Edith's distress) and scientific materialism (represented by Art's stubborn skepticism).

**The Power of Story:** All of reality occurs through dialogue and interpretation. John's tale could be a elaborate hoax, a delusion, or literally true—the film deliberately refuses to confirm which interpretation is correct, leaving viewers to decide what they believe.

**Low-Budget Innovation:** The film's entire premise is talk-driven, occurring in one location with minimal visual effects. This constraint became a strength, demonstrating that science fiction doesn't require spectacle—it can work through intellectual engagement and philosophical speculation.

entries.title: The Man from Planet X
entries.creator: Edgar G. Ulmer
entries.pitch: The Man from Planet X is a independently made 1951 American black-and-white science fiction horror film that represents one of the earliest depictions of alien contact in cinema. Directed by Edgar G. Ulmer and produced by Jack Pollexfen and Aubrey Wisberg, the film stars Robert Clarke, Margaret Field, and William Schallert. The story concerns a humanoid alien who lands on Earth in a spaceship from a mysterious planet and makes contact with a small group of humans on an isolated, fog-shrouded Scottish moor. With a modest budget of $51,000, the film went on to gross $1.2 million, proving that low-budget sci-fi could find audiences during the genre's golden age of the early 1950s.
entries.truth:
A spaceship from a previously unknown planet lands in the Scottish moorlands near the observatory of Professor Elliot. The humanoid alien that emerges is initially peaceful, communicating through musical tones. American reporter John Lawrence and the Professor attempt to establish contact but fail. Meanwhile, the unscrupulous scientist Dr. Mears discovers the alien's ability to communicate and attempts to force from it the metal formula for its spaceship. He shuts off the alien's breathing apparatus and leaves the spaceman for dead, telling the professor that communication was hopeless.

When Lawrence discovers that the alien has vanished along with Professor Elliot's daughter Enid and other villagers, he realizes something far more sinister is happening. With more villagers missing and the phone lines dead, word finally reaches Scotland Yard via heliograph. As military forces arrive, it becomes clear that the alien intended to use its ship as a wireless relay station in advance of an invasion from its dying home planet. With the planet due to reach its closest approach to Earth at midnight, Lawrence must act to rescue the possessed villagers before the invasion begins.
entries.core:
The Man from Planet X stands as a pioneering work in the alien invasion subgenre, predating many of the more famous films that would define the decade. Its depiction of a dying alien world sending advance scouts to Earth established tropes that would become genre staples. The film's low-budget constraints actually work in its favor—the fog-shrouded sets and minimalist alien design create genuine atmosphere and dread.

The film also reflects early 1950s anxieties about the unknown during the Cold War era. The idea of an alien invasion force preparing to conquer Earth resonated with fears of infiltration and enemy advance. The mysterious planet from a dying world serves as both literal threat and metaphor for the uncertainty of the atomic age.

Edgar G. Ulmer's direction maximizes the production's limited resources, using shadow and mist to create an eerie atmosphere that transcends the modest budget. The film's influence can be seen in subsequent alien contact narratives, and it remains a cult classic among science fiction film historians for its historical significance and atmospheric storytelling.

entries.title: The Man from the First Century
entries.creator: Oldřich Lipský
entries.pitch:
The Man from the First Century (original Czech title: Muž z prvního století, also known as Man in Outer Space) is a 1962 Czechoslovak science fiction comedy directed by Oldřich Lipský. The film was entered into the 1962 Cannes Film Festival, marking one of the few Eastern Bloc sci-fi comedies to receive international recognition during the Cold War.

The story follows Josef, an upholsterer working on the first Czech spacecraft, who accidentally launches the vessel during final adjustments and finds himself transported to the planet Blue Star. Upon arriving, he encounters Adam, an inhabitant of this distant world, and the two return together to Earth—only to discover that 600 years have passed. Josef is initially institutionalized as a delusional psychiatric patient, but escapes with Adam's help, who proves invisible to contemporary Earthlings. Together, they work to return Josef to his own time.
entries.truth:
Set in the 20th century, the Czechoslovak space program is preparing to launch its first spacecraft.
