This 1978 TV movie offers a grimly prescient warning about corporate militarism and technological apocalypse, reimagining Wells' classic for a Cold War audience increasingly anxious about nuclear annihilation and corporate power.
entries.truth:
Dr. Neil Perry works for theMega Corporation, a powerful defense contractor. His genius has secured a $20 million grant to build a time machine, but when the project nears completion, his superiors order him to abandon it in favor of developing an antimatter bomb - a weapon of mass destruction. 

When the power module completes earlier than expected, Perry uses his machine to travel through time. His first journey takes him to 1692 Salem, Massachusetts, where he's accused of witchcraft and barely escapes being burned at the stake. He then detours to 1871 during the California Gold Rush, where he's mistaken for a robber and arrested.

Returning to his own time, Perry is disturbed by reports of environmental destruction caused by Mega's weapons. He travels to the future to gather proof, witnessing the fiery apocalypse that consumes civilization in 2025. Humanity retreats underground, eventually evolving into two species: the Eloi, gentle surface-dwellers, and the Morlocks, subterranean monsters who farm the Eloi for food.

In the far future, Perry befriends Weena, an Eloi, and her companion Ariel. Together with other Eloi, they use plastic explosives from a museum to seal the Morlocks' tunnels, freeing the Eloi from predation. Perry returns to his own time with evidence of the catastrophe to come.

Back in the present, Perry presents his findings to the Mega board. While his colleague Branly believes him, executives Haverson and Worthington are only interested in weaponizing time travel for military advantage. Disgusted, Perry, Branly, and secretary Agnes leave Mega Corporation. Perry chooses to return to the future - to Weena and the freed Eloi - rather than remain in a world careening toward destruction.
entries.core:
The 1978 Time Machine is a product of its era - a cautionary tale about the military-industrial complex, environmental destruction, and the unintended consequences of technological advancement. The film updates Wells' Victorian warning about class division for the nuclear age, replacing the industrial worker/capitalist dynamic with a corporate/military apparatus that enables humanity's ultimate self-destruction.

The Morlocks represent humanity's potential devolution - those who retreated underground, who embraced darkness and predation. The Eloi represent the survivors, gentle and innocent but vulnerable. This reflects anxieties about nuclear war and environmental collapse that defined late 1970s discourse. Perry's journey is ultimately futile in preventing the catastrophe, but he can help the survivors in the aftermath.

The film also critiques corporate power and militarism, presenting Mega Corporation as an amoral entity willing to destroy the world for profit. The antimatter bomb project mirrors real-world concerns about nuclear proliferation and the military-industrial complex that Eisenhower warned about in his farewell address.

What makes this version distinctive is its TV movie aesthetic - low-budget and earnest, with practical effects that give the Morlocks and Eloi an unsettling, almost surreal quality. It's a forgotten artifact of 1970s futurism, less polished than George Pal's 1960 version but more directly political in its critique of American corporate and military power.

entries.title: The Time Ships
entries.creator: Stephen Baxter
entries.pitch: The Time Ships is a canonical sequel to H.G. Wells' seminal 1895 novella The Time Machine, officially authorized by the Wells estate to mark the centenary of the original's publication. The novel picks up immediately after the events of The Time Machine, with the Time Traveler attempting to return to the year 802,701 to save Weena, the Eloi who perished in the fire. However, upon arriving in the future, he discovers that his own story—the publication of The Time Machine—has altered the timeline, leading to a radically different future. The Time Traveler must navigate a world where humanity has evolved alongside the Morlocks in unexpected ways, including a Dyson sphere surrounding the Sun. This is hard science fiction at its finest, exploring time travel, human evolution, and the butterfly effect with rigorous scientific thinking.
entries.truth:
After the events of The Time Machine, the Time Traveler (whose first name Moses is revealed in the novel) returns to 1891 to prepare for another journey back to the year 802,701. He hopes to save Weena, the Eloi who died in the fire with the Morlocks. The time machine's quartz construction is now suffused with a radioactive substance called Plattnerite, given to him by a mysterious benefactor twenty years earlier.

The Time Traveler departs into the future but stops in 657,208 when he notices the daytime sky has gone permanently dark. He arrives and is captured by a more culturally advanced branch of Morlocks. One of them, Nebogipfel (a character from Wells' earlier story "The Chronic Argonauts"), explains that the conflict between Eloi and Morlocks never occurred because the Writer (Wells himself) published the story that became The Time Machine, altering history. The timeline the Time Traveler sought no longer exists.

In this new timeline, the Morlocks have constructed a Dyson sphere around the inner Solar System, harnessing the Sun's energy. Humans live on the sunlit inner surface of the Sphere while the Morlocks inhabit the outer shell. The Time Traveler convinces Nebogipfel to help him understand the time travel mechanism, and the Morlock agrees to take him back to his machine.

The story then follows the Time Traveler through multiple epochs of human evolution and cosmic engineering, including visits to a future where humanity has merged with machines, a universe where reality itself is being rewritten, and the ultimate fate of consciousness across cosmic time. The novel explores themes of determinism versus free will, the nature of time itself, and what it means to be human in an ever-changing universe.
entries.core:
The Time Ships is fundamentally about the nature of time, causality, and the power of stories to shape reality. By making H.G. Wells himself a character in the novel and having the publication of The Time Machine alter the timeline, Baxter explores how science fiction can serve as a form of temporal prophecy—how imagining the future can help create it.

The novel also examines the relationship between humans and technology through the lens of hard science fiction. The Dyson sphere, the time machine's mechanics, and the various post-human entities the Time Traveler encounters all serve to question what humanity truly is and whether our biological origins matter in the face of technological transformation.

Baxter's rigorous approach to physics—time dilation, relativity, thermodynamics—grounds the more speculative elements in scientific plausibility. Yet the novel never loses sight of its emotional core: the Time Traveler's grief over losing Weena and his desperate attempt to change the past. This combination of hard science and deep human emotion makes The Time Ships a worthy successor to Wells' original masterpiece.

The novel also serves as a meditation on legacy and memory. As the Time Traveler journeys further into the future, he becomes increasingly aware that he is the last living person who remembers the world as it was, a lone anchor to a past that no longer exists in any form.

entries.title: The Time Travelers
entries.creator: Ib Melchior
entries.pitch: The Time Travelers is a 1964 American science fiction film directed by Ib Melchior, featuring Preston Foster, Philip Carey, and Merry Anders. The film follows a group of scientists testing a time-viewing device that suddenly becomes a portal through time, drawing enormous power from the electrical grid. When technician Danny McKee steps through the portal, the others follow to rescue him, only to find themselves stranded in a devastated future where nuclear war has rendered Earth uninhabitable. The survivors must work with the remaining human colony to rebuild their time device while hostile mutants threaten their survival. The film was released by American International Pictures and inspired the TV series "The Time Tunnel."
entries.truth:
Scientists Dr. Erik von Steiner, Dr. Steve Connors, and Carol White are testing their time-viewing device at a research facility when it begins drawing excessive power. Technician Danny McKee arrives to warn them to shut down, but during the test, odd shadows appear and the screen reveals a stark, barren landscape. Danny discovers the screen has become a "time portal" and steps through, disappearing into the future.

The portal becomes unstable, and the others enter it to rescue Danny. They find themselves in a hostile wasteland, pursued by mutated survivors of nuclear war. They take refuge in an underground city—the last remnants of civilization in the year A.D. 2071. The city's leader, Dr. Varno, explains that Earth can no longer support life, and the colonists are frantically building a spacecraft to flee to a planet orbiting Alpha Centauri.

The time travelers learn that the mutants, once peaceful, have become increasingly aggressive as resources dwindled. The future humans offer to help rebuild the time portal so the travelers can return home. However, just as they complete the device, the mutants attack and destroy the spacecraft during liftoff. The only escape is to return through the time portal.

Back in their own era, the travelers realize their calibration was off—they're experiencing time at an accelerated rate and will die of old age by day's end. Their only option is to travel to the date the portal had been set to before: over 100,000 years in the future. They enter the portal and find a sunlit, habitable Earth, while their past selves repeat the same actions in an accelerating cycle that ends with a shot of the Andromeda Galaxy.
entries.core:
The Time Travelers exemplifies 1960s American science fiction's preoccupations with nuclear annihilation and humanity's survival beyond Earth. The film presents a future Earth destroyed by nuclear war—a common trope of the atomic age—but offers an unusual twist: rather than escaping to space, the survivors have been forced underground, and their attempt to colonize another planet fails catastrophically.

The film's themes explore temporal paradox, humanity's resilience, and the cyclical nature of history. The ending, with its ambiguous suggestion of cyclical time and regeneration, hints at philosophical questions about fate and possibility. The time travelers' choice to face an unknown future rather than remain in their dying present speaks to the human impulse toward hope even in extremis.

Director Ib Melchior, known for his work in B-movie science fiction, crafted a thoughtful entry in the time travel genre that influenced later productions, including the creation of Irwin Allen's "The Time Tunnel" television series. The film's modest budget leads to some creative solutions, including using conjuring tricks for android effects, but the conceptual ambition—depicting multiple time periods and a post-apocalyptic future—demonstrates the era's optimistic faith in science fiction's imaginative possibilities.

The Time Travelers remains notable for its early treatment of time travel mechanics and post-apocalyptic survival, themes that would become genre staples in subsequent decades.

entries.title: The Tragedy of Man
entries.creator: Marcell Jankovics (Director)
entries.pitch: The Tragedy of Man is a 2011 Hungarian adult animated epic drama spanning 160 minutes, directed by Marcell Jankovics. Based on Imre Madách's 1861 philosophical play, the film follows Adam, Eve, and Lucifer as they journey through human history—from Biblical creation to 50,000 BC to the distant future—exploring humanity's purpose and the meaning of life. Each segment features a distinct visual style reflecting the art of different historical periods. After 23 years in production, this ambitious animated work stands as one of the longest animated films ever made.
entries.truth:
The film opens with God creating the universe, as Lucifer mocks humanity's shortcomings and demands his share of the world. Granted the Tree of Knowledge and Tree of Immortality, he tempts Adam and Eve to eat the forbidden fruit, leading to their expulsion from Eden.

Lucifer then offers Adam the chance to experience humanity's future. The journey spans multiple historical eras: ancient Egypt where Adam becomes Pharaoh Djoser; ancient Greece during thePeloponnesian War; ancient Rome at the height of the Empire; medieval times as a Crusader knight; Renaissance Prague as Johannes Kepler; Revolutionary France as Georges Danton; Victorian London; World War I era; and finally a dystopian future world state in the 21st century.

Throughout these episodes, Eve appears in various forms—slave woman, aristocrat, prostitute, mother—while Lucifer argues that humanity is fundamentally futile. Adam consistently finds disillusionment: democracy becomes corruption, civilization falls into decadence, and scientific progress leads to dehumanization. In the future, humanity has eliminated nations, genetically modified all life, and suppresses individual thought.

In the distant ice age, the last humans are dying as deformed savages. Adam, despairing, considers suicide but is convinced to continue by Eve's pregnancy announcement. God urges faith in humanity's struggle, and Adam accepts mortality as meaningful.
entries.core:
The film is a profound philosophical meditation on free will, faith, and human perseverance. Jankovics adapts Madách's enduring question: is humanity worth creating despite its suffering? The answer emerges not through cosmic meaning but through acceptance of struggle itself—existence as its own purpose.

The animation's varying styles brilliantly mirror historical periods, from Egyptian hieroglyphics to Renaissance painting to abstract futurism. The classical music soundtrack—featuring works by Bach, Wagner, Mussorgsky, and Liszt—elevates the philosophical weight.

The film tackles eternal questions: Does progress improve humanity or merely change its forms of suffering? Is free will real or illusory? Can meaning exist without divine guarantee? Rather than providing answers, the film suggests that the struggle itself—continuing despite uncertainty—constitutes authentic human existence.

After 23 years in production, The Tragedy of Man represents a unique achievement in animated philosophy, joining the ranks of ambitious animated works that use the medium for profound thematic exploration rather than mere entertainment.

entries.title: The Truth of Fact, the Truth of Feeling
entries.creator: Ted Chiang
entries.pitch: In a near-future world where life's moments can be recorded and replayed at will, a man struggles with the unreliability of his own memories against the objective truth captured on video. Meanwhile, in 19th-century Tanzania, an anthropologist documents the oral traditions of the Sandawe people, discovering how stories evolve through generations. These two narratives intertwine to explore a profound question: What is the relationship between recorded facts and felt truth? Can the objective capture of a moment ever capture what it truly meant to be there? Chiang weaves these parallel stories into a meditation on memory, identity, and the stories we tell ourselves about who we are.
entries.truth:
The story operates on two timelines:

**The Future Narrative:** Marcus, a middle-aged man living in a world where everyone records their daily lives through "lifelogging" devices, watches an old video of his teenage daughter and realizes he has completely forgotten a meaningful moment they shared. The recording shows him laughing at something his daughter said, but he has no memory of this at all. This triggers an existential crisis—he begins to question whether his memories, which feel so visceral and true, are actually accurate representations of his life. His wife, a therapist, tries to reassure him that memories are not meant to be perfect recordings but rather the emotional truth of experiences. However, Marcus becomes obsessed with comparing his memories to his lifelogs, discovering discrepancies that disturb him deeply.

**The Historical Narrative:** In the 1870s, Rev. J.C. M., a missionary and linguist, travels to Tanzania to study the language of the Sandawe people. He works closely with a Sandawe man named Relana, who helps him document their oral traditions. Rev. M. discovers that the Sandawe have no written language but maintain a sophisticated system of oral history through song. He becomes obsessed with recording their stories exactly as told, believing he's preserving "pure" truth. However, he fails to understand that the Sandawe see their oral traditions as living, evolving things—stories are meant to be adapted, not frozen. When he publishes his work, he presents his translations as definitive, not realizing he's captured only a moment in an ongoing process of cultural evolution.

**The Connection:** The two narratives converge thematically around the question of how we preserve truth. Marcus realizes that his memories, while imperfect, contain emotional truths that recordings cannot capture. He understands that the act of forgetting is not failure but part of how humans make meaning from experience. Similarly, Rev. M.'s work, though "accurate" in its factual recording, missed the essential truth of oral tradition—that stories are meant to be felt, not just preserved. The story ends with both men coming to terms with the limitations of pure factual recording and the importance of emotional truth.
entries.core:
Chiang explores the tension between **objectivity and subjectivity** in human experience. Lifelogging technology promises perfect memory, but the story argues that perfect factual记录 is not the same as meaningful truth. Our memories are not video files—they're constructed narratives that give experiences their emotional weight. Without the imperfection of memory, we might lose the ability to find meaning in our pasts.

The story also examines **cultural memory and oral tradition**. Rev. M.'s failure to understand Sandawe oral history reveals a Western assumption that truth must be fixed and recorded to be valid. The Sandawe understanding—that truth lives in the telling, in the relationship between storyteller and audience—offers an alternative model of how truth functions in human communities.

Most profoundly, the story asks: **What makes something "true"?** Is it accurate factual representation, or is it the emotional reality of experience? Chiang suggests that both kinds of truth matter, but they are not the same. A recording can capture what happened without capturing what it meant. And sometimes, the stories we tell ourselves about our pasts—imperfect as they are—contain more truth than any objective record could.

This is quintessential Chiang—rigorous science fiction premises deployed in service of profound philosophical questions about human existence. The story doesn't offer easy answers but invites readers to consider their own relationships with memory, truth, and the narratives that shape their identities.

entries.title: The Tunnel
entries.creator: William Wauer (director), Bernhard Kellermann (novel)
entries.pitch: The Tunnel is a 1915 German silent science fiction film, one of the earliest feature-length sci-fi movies ever made. Directed by William Wauer, it adapts Bernhard Kellermann's 1913 novel about an ambitious engineering project that captures the world's imagination: the construction of a massive tunnel under the Atlantic Ocean connecting Europe and America. The film survives today—a rare feat for silent era cinema—and was restored in 2010, allowing modern audiences to witness this pioneering work of speculative fiction. Notably, Adolf Hitler reportedly found the film and novel influential, seeing in them a demonstration of how oratory and mass persuasion could shape historical events.
entries.truth:
The film follows the story of the construction of the Atlantic Tunnel, an enormous engineering endeavor that promises to revolutionize global connectivity by connecting Europe and America via an underwater passage. The project becomes a global sensation, with massive public interest, political debates, and the mobilization of unprecedented resources.

The narrative focuses on the challenges faced by the engineers and workers as they attempt to bore through the ocean floor, dealing with geological obstacles, financial difficulties, and the immense psychological toll of working in such an unprecedented environment. The tunnel represents not just an engineering achievement but a symbol of human ambition and the desire to conquer nature.

As the project progresses, it attracts the attention of governments, financiers, and the public worldwide. The film depicts the social and political implications of such a project—international cooperation, competition between nations, and the transformation of global commerce and communication.

The story also explores the human drama surrounding the tunnel: the lives of workers, the families waiting for their return, and the visionaries who believed in the impossible. The film was notable for its elaborate sets and special effects for the era, depicting the tunnel boring machines and the underwater construction in ways that must have seemed miraculous to 1915 audiences.
entries.core:
**Pioneering Spirit and Technological Optimism:** The Tunnel represents early cinema's fascination with technological progress. At a time when major engineering projects like the Panama Canal were capturing global attention, the film tapped into contemporary optimism about what human ingenuity could accomplish. The tunnel becomes a metaphor for human achievement against seemingly impossible odds.

**International Cooperation and Competition:** The transatlantic tunnel would fundamentally reshape global relations, and the film explores how such a project affects international politics. It reflects early 20th-century debates about globalization, national competitiveness, and the promise of technology to bring nations together—or create new divisions.

**The Power of Vision and Persuasion:** The historical note about Hitler's reaction to the film is significant—it suggests the film was understood even in its own time as a work about the power of grand visions to mobilize masses. The tunnel is not just infrastructure but a symbol that can inspire, unify, or manipulate populations.

**Survival and Restoration:** The fact that this film survived while so many others from the era have been lost makes its existence itself meaningful. Its 2010 restoration ensures that this piece of sci-fi cinema history remains accessible, allowing us to see how early filmmakers imagined the future.

The Tunnel stands as a foundational work in science fiction cinema—a silent era speculation about technological ambition that resonates with modern discussions about mega-projects, global connectivity, and the dreams (and dangers) of engineering the impossible.

entries.title: The Tunnel
entries.creator: Curtis Bernhardt
entries.pitch:
The Tunnel is a 1933 German science fiction film adapted from Bernhard Kellermann's 1913 novel, depicting the ambitious engineering project to construct a transatlantic tunnel connecting Europe and America. Directed by Curtis Bernhardt and starring Paul Hartmann, Attila Hörbiger, and Gustaf Gründgens, the film presents an early vision of global connectivity and the technological optimism of the early 20th century—before the shadow of World War II would darken such visions of progress.

The story follows Mac Allen, the chief engineer leading the massive undertaking to bore a tunnel beneath the Atlantic Ocean. Facing financial crises, political opposition, and the immense technical challenges of such an unprecedented project, Allen and his team press forward despite mounting obstacles. The film explores the tension between visionary ambition and the practical, political, and financial forces that threaten to derail great endeavors.
entries.truth:
The narrative centers on the construction of the transatlantic tunnel, following the engineering team led by Mac Allen as they attempt to complete the monumental project. The tunnel syndicate, headed by Mr. Woolf (played by Gustaf Gründgens), provides the financial backing, but the project faces constant threats from financiers who question the returns, from the elements themselves, and from those who oppose such a radical transformation of the world order.

The film depicts the technical challenges of boring through the ocean floor, the political machinations surrounding funding and international agreements, and the personal dramas of the engineers and financiers involved. A separate French version was also produced, and in 1935 Britain would remake the film as "Transatlantic Tunnel," released in the U.S. under that same title.

The cast includes notable actors of the German cinema of the era, including Max Schreck in a supporting role, adding another connection to the Expressionist horror tradition that defined much of German filmmaking in the 1920s and early 1930s.
entries.core:
The Tunnel represents a fascinating artifact of early 1930s German cinema, capturing the technological optimism of the era while also reflecting the anxieties of the period. The transatlantic tunnel serves as a metaphor for human ambition and the desire to connect distant peoples—themes that resonate particularly strongly in the context of 1930s Europe, where both the promise and peril of large-scale engineering projects were very much in evidence.

The film can be seen as part of a tradition of German speculative fiction that includes Metropolis (1927), exploring humanity's relationship with technology and large-scale projects. Like Metropolis, The Tunnel presents technology as both transformative and potentially dangerous, asking whether the benefits of grand engineering projects justify the costs—financial, human, and political.

The 1933 release date is significant: this was a transitional moment in German history, and the film's themes of ambitious projects, financial intrigue, and the struggle between visionaries and conservative forces take on added resonance in this context. The film's production at Bavaria Film and its subsequent international success (including the French version and British remake) also speak to the transnational nature of early science fiction cinema.

Today, The Tunnel remains an important early entry in the science fiction film genre, demonstrating that the genre's fascination with megaprojects and technological ambition was alive and well even in the early days of the form.

entries.title: The Unpleasant Profession of Jonathan Hoag
entries.creator: Robert A. Heinlein (as "John Riverside")
entries.pitch:
Jonathan Hoag is a Chicago art enthusiast who discovers he has no memory of his daytime occupation—and finds mysterious red-brown substance under his fingernails that might be blood. When he hires detectives Ted and Cynthia Randall to follow him during the day, they encounter something far stranger than a missing job: a reality that doesn't quite hold together, where fingerprints vanish, buildings have no thirteenth floor, and ancient pagan beings called the "Sons of the Bird" pull strings from behind the scenes.

This 1942 science fantasy novella reads like a lost Philip K. Dick novel—part noir mystery, part reality-bending philosophical puzzle. Heinlein (writing under a pseudonym) delivers a haunting meditation on the nature of reality, free will, and whether we are merely characters in someone else's artistic experiment. The story has been described by critics as "the last and strangest of the stories that Robert Heinlein contributed to the Golden Age."
entries.truth:
Jonathan Hoag, a wealthy Chicago man about town known for his refined tastes in art and dining, realizes during dinner one evening that he cannot remember what he does during the day. More disturbingly, when he washes his hands, he discovers a reddish-brown substance under his fingernails that looks like dried blood.

He hires the detective agency Randall & Craig—actually the married couple Ted and Cynthia Randall—to follow him and discover his daytime identity. The investigation quickly turns bizarre: Hoag leaves no fingerprints, his memories of his job are false, and when the Randalls tail him into an office building, they experience conflicting realities. Cynthia sees Hoag turn and speak to her husband in an empty hallway; Ted discovers Hoag is actually a jeweler working for Detheridge & Co., and the red substance is simply jeweler's rouge used for polishing gems.

The couple encounters increasingly strange phenomena: Hoag menacing Cynthia in an elevator, the building mysteriously lacking a thirteenth floor, and Ted experiencing vivid "dreams" where men in business suits warn him to leave Hoag alone. These businessmen reveal themselves as the "Sons of the Bird," disciples of an ancient pagan religion who regard Hoag as their enemy.

When Cynthia is taken through a mirror into the Sons' realm, something is extracted from her and sealed in a bottle, leaving her in a comatose state. Ted paints over all mirrors in their apartment to prevent further intrusion and calls upon the eccentric Dr. Potiphar T. Potbury for help—only to discover Potbury is also one of the Sons.

Eventually, the truth emerges through drug interrogation: Jonathan Hoag reveals a dominant second personality and explains that our entire world is an art project created by a student-artist. Ted, Cynthia, and Hoag himself are "critics" who live within the world to judge experiences—eating, drinking, loving—without knowing they are observers. The Sons of the Bird were an artistic mistake, hastily "painted over" during the creation but still retaining power.

Hoag completes his mission to expose and eliminate the Sons, then "returns" to the artistic plane, leaving his physical body inert. The Randalls flee but find that the world outside has become a pulsing, luminous mist—the boundaries of the created reality closing behind them.

In the epilogue, the Randalls live in remote isolation by the sea, with no mirrors in their home and Ted handcuffing himself to Cynthia each night to prevent either of them from vanishing in the night.
entries.core:
This novella operates on multiple philosophical levels. At its surface, it's a noir-inflected mystery thriller about a man who doesn't know himself—but beneath that, Heinlein poses profound questions about the nature of reality.

The story predates (and anticipated) many themes that would later define existentialist science fiction: Are we characters in someone else's story? Can we trust our perceptions of reality? The "art critic" revelation—that human experience is merely raw material for artistic judgment—challenges the reader to consider whether consciousness, free will, and meaning are genuine or merely borrowed from a creator's design.

The Sons of the Bird represent the glitches in any system—the forgotten code that still holds power, the suppressed aspects of reality that refuse to stay buried. They also function as a critique of religious institutions as "artistic mistakes" that took on a life of their own.

Heinlein's choice to publish under the pseudonym "John Riverside" and the story's strange tone (unlike his typical juvenile sci-fi) suggest he knew he was producing something unusual—a meditation on creativity, perception, and the boundaries of fiction itself that feels decades ahead of its time.

The ending—with its mirrored handcuffs, no mirrors in the house, and the luminous mist at the world's edge—suggests that even after "escaping" the Sons, the Randalls remain trapped in the artwork, perhaps now aware of their status as characters in an unfinished work. It's a profoundly unsettling conclusion that rivals Dick's best paranoid realities.

entries.title: The Vanishing Shadow
entries.creator: Lew Landers
entries.pitch: The Vanishing Shadow is a 1934 Universal Pictures science fiction film serial that holds the distinction of featuring what is believed to be the first appearance of a hand-held ray gun in film history. Directed by Lew Landers, this 12-chapter serial follows inventor Stanley Stanfield, who creates the "Vanishing Ray"—a revolutionary wearable device that renders the wearer invisible, leaving only their shadow visible. When Stanley inherits valuable bonds from his late father, a corrupt stockbroker and the villainous businessman Wade Barnett stop at nothing to acquire them. Adding complexity to the conflict is Stanley's love interest Gloria Grant, who is secretly the estranged daughter of his enemy. The serial is notable for its array of science fiction gadgets, including a robot and "The Destroying Ray," making it a pioneering work in the sci-fi serial genre.
entries.truth:
The story begins with Stanley Stanfield demonstrating his invention, the Vanishing Ray, to fellow scientist Carl Van Dorn. Stanley intends to sell bonds to finance the invention's development. However, the stockbroker he meets is corruptly aligned with Wade Barnett, a ruthless businessman who previously hounded Stanley's father to his death. Barnett wants the bonds and will use any means necessary to obtain them.

Stanley inherits the bonds from his late father, who was the publisher and editor of the local Tribune newspaper. This sets off a dangerous game of cat and mouse between Stanley and Barnett's henchmen. Complicating matters is Gloria Grant, Stanley's girlfriend, who is actually Gloria Barnett—Wade's estranged daughter who changed her name. Neither hero nor villain wants to see Gloria hurt, forcing both to work around this delicate situation throughout the 12-chapter serial.

The serial features several science fiction elements that were groundbreaking for its time. Beyond the Vanishing Ray and handheld ray gun, the story includes a robot and "The Destroying Ray" as key plot devices. The chapters include: Accused of Murder, The Destroying Ray, The Avalanche, Trapped, Hurled from the Sky, Chain Lightning, The Tragic Crash, The Shadow of Death, Blazing Bulkheads, The Iron Death, The Juggernaut, and Retribution.

In the end, Barnett's henchman Dorgan captures both Stanley and Gloria but ultimately blackmail his boss to ensure her safety. During the confrontation, Barnett is shot and killed. Before dying, he reconciles with his daughter. Gloria and Stanley marry and take over operation of the Tribune newspaper.

Notably, for decades the serial was believed to be lost, with only a short nitrate trailer surviving in the George Eastman House archive. In 2010, all 12 chapters were uploaded to YouTube, and in 2019 it was released on DVD.
entries.core:
The Vanishing Shadow represents an important milestone in early science fiction cinema, particularly within the film serial format that was popular during the 1930s. The serial's claim to featuring the first handheld ray gun in film is significant, as this would become one of science fiction's most iconic weapons in subsequent decades of cinema.

The film reflects the era's fascination with "mad scientist" tropes and inventive technology, but also introduces themes that would become staples of the genre: the genius inventor, the corrupt businessman antagonist, and the struggle between technology and moral use. The Vanishing Ray itself—invisible technology that leaves only a shadow—is a imaginative concept that predates many similar ideas in later science fiction.

The serial format, with its chapter-by-chapter cliffhangers, was the dominant way science fiction stories reached audiences in the pre-television era. These serials helped establish many visual and narrative conventions that would influence science fiction films for decades to come. The Vanishing Shadow stands as a document of this formative period in science fiction media history.

entries.title: The Velvet Prison
entries.creator: Stephen R. Donaldson
entries.pitch: In a future society where war, conflict, and emotional suffering have been eliminated through a daily drug called "Mello," citizens live in perfect contentment. The protagonist is an artist who has chosen to forego the drug, maintaining his emotional range—including the painful parts—but finding it increasingly difficult to survive in a world of permanent, drug-induced serenity. When his art attracts attention from the authorities, he faces a devastating choice: conform to the velvet prison of numbness, or fight to preserve the messy, painful, glorious spectrum of human emotion. This Hugo Award-winning novelette is a piercing meditation on freedom, happiness, and the true cost of peace.
entries.truth:
The story is set in a future where virtually all citizens take a medication called "Mello" that suppresses negative emotions—anger, fear, sadness, jealousy, and even passion. The result is a society of profound peace and contentment, where violence and conflict have been virtually eliminated. People live in a state of gentle, pleasant detachment, fulfilled but emotionally shallow.

The protagonist is an artist who has chosen to stop taking Mello. He experiences the full range of human emotions—the highs and the devastating lows—but this makes him a legal anomaly and a threat to social order. He paints, creating works that express the emotional depth the others have surrendered, but he must hide his identity and his art.

His existence becomes increasingly precarious. The authorities, represented by Well-Beings who monitor compliance with the emotional regulation, are concerned about "unattached" individuals—people who refuse Mello. The protagonist's work begins to circulate, and its emotional power disturbs those who see it, triggering faint echoes of feelings they've never known.

The story reaches its climax when the protagonist is finally confronted. He's offered a choice: return to taking Mello and be reintegrated into society, or face exile to a "reservation" where other non-compliant people supposedly live freely. The twist reveals that the reservations are actually places where people are left to die—the government simply can't execute people directly, so they offer what sounds like freedom but is actually a slow death.

The protagonist must make an impossible choice: accept the "velvet prison" of emotional suppression and live comfortably but without authentic feeling, or reject it and face a kind of death. The story ends ambiguously but powerfully, leaving the reader to contemplate the nature of freedom and happiness.
entries.core:
Donaldson explores the fundamental freedom, asking whether tension between security and peace achieved through the suppression of human nature is truly peace at all. The "velvet prison" is velvet precisely because it's comfortable—it feels good not to suffer, not to feel jealousy or anger. But that comfort comes at the cost of authenticity, passion, and the full richness of human experience.

The story is also a sharp critique of utilitarianism and the idea that the greatest good for the greatest number justifies stripping away what makes us human. The citizens of this world are happy, but their happiness is chemically induced and emotionally shallow. Donaldson suggests that suffering and joy are two sides of the same coin—you cannot have one without the risk of the other.

Additionally, the story examines the role of art in society. The protagonist's art is dangerous precisely because it evokes emotion in those who view it, waking them to feelings they've surrendered. Art, Donaldson implies, serves as a repository of authentic human experience, preserving what society might otherwise erase.

The title itself is perfectly apt: the prison is made of velvet—soft, luxurious, comfortable—but it's still a prison. The greatest danger isn't oppression that's harsh and obvious, but the subtle seduction of comfort that steals our freedom without our noticing.

entries.title: The Visitor
entries.pitch: The Visitor is a 1979 Italian-American science fiction horror film directed by Giulio Paradisi. Set in Atlanta, Georgia, the film tells the story of a battle between cosmic forces representing good and evil, fought over the soul of a young girl with telekinetic abilities. The film features an unusual cast including Mel Ferrer, Glenn Ford, Lance Henriksen, John Huston, Sam Peckinpah, and Shelley Winters. Produced as an international co-production between Italian and American companies, it was filmed at Cinecittà Studios in Rome and on location in Atlanta. The film has become a cult classic over the years, known for its unusual premise and surreal imagery.
entries.truth:
The film opens with Jerzy Colsowicz experiencing a vision of a destructive storm brought about by a young human girl. He learns from a Christ-like figure about a centuries-long cosmic conflict between Zatteen, an evil inter-spatial force with powerful psychic abilities, and his benevolent rival Yahweh. Zatteen escaped to Earth centuries ago and was killed, but his spirit lives on in the minds of humankind, waiting to reemerge.

Zatteen had produced dozens of children with human women before his death, and these descendants continue to populate Earth. At a basketball game in Atlanta, home team owner Raymond Armstead is revealed to be in league with a secret group of Satanists who wish to bring about Zatteen's resurgence. Their plan involves using Barbara Collins, whose eight-year-old daughter Katy has displayed psychokinetic abilities, as a conduit to distill Zatteen's powers into a corporeal human form.

Colsowicz, who possesses similar powers to Katy, is sent to Earth to stop the Satanists. He attempts to save Katy from the influence of evil while the Satanists attempt to have Raymond father a male child with Barbara, who would then mate with his half-sister to produce the physical embodiment of Zatteen. The film culminates in a battle between the cosmic forces, with Colsowitz intervening to stop the Satanists and save Katy from becoming the vessel for Zatteen's return.
entries.core:
The Visitor stands as a unique entry in the sci-fi horror genre, blending religious mythology with paranormal themes. The film draws on Abrahamic religious imagery—depicting the cosmic struggle between God and Satan—as the framework for its science fiction premise. This religious science fiction approach was somewhat unusual for the late 1970s, when the genre typically favored either secular alien invasion narratives or purely supernatural horror.

The film also touches on themes of psychic powers and inherited abilities, predating the surge of similar themes in 1980s cinema. Young Katy Collins represents the "chosen vessel" archetype, but with a twist—she is unknowingly aligned with evil forces rather than being a traditional chosen one hero.

The ensemble cast, featuring several Hollywood legends in unconventional roles, has been a point of discussion among cult film enthusiasts. The film has been noted for its bizarre tone, which shifts between genuine horror sequences and almost absurdist moments. Despite—or perhaps because of—its oddities, The Visitor has developed a cult following, appreciated for its distinctive vision and the way it weaves religious mythology into a sci-fi horror narrative.

entries.title: The Voice from the Sky
entries.creator: Ben F. Wilson
entries.pitch: The Voice from the Sky is a 1930 American science fiction film serial directed by Ben F. Wilson, notable as the first serial film to feature full sound. The story follows Jack Deering (Wally Wales), a U.S. Secret Service agent who investigates a mysterious figure known only as "The Man from Nowhere" – a cloaked stranger who appears in small towns, performing seemingly miraculous deeds before vanishing. As Deering delves deeper into the mystery, he uncovers a plot involving a brilliant scientist, Edgar Ballin (Robert Walker), who has developed revolutionary technology. The serial blends science fiction elements with adventure and mystery, featuring episodes like "Doomed," "The Cave of Horrors," and "The Pit of Peril." Considered a lost film, it remains an important piece of early sci-fi cinema history.
entries.truth:
The serial opens with the mysterious appearance of a hooded figure in various towns across America. This "Man from Nowhere" performs strange acts—curing the sick, helping the poor, and demonstrating abilities beyond normal human capability. Meanwhile, Jack Deering, a Secret Service agent, is assigned to investigate these strange occurrences and the identity of this enigmatic figure.

Deering's investigation leads him to Jean Lovell (Neva Gerber), whose father was a renowned scientist. Through her, he discovers that Edgar Ballin, her father's former colleague, has developed extraordinary technology and may be behind the Man from Nowhere. Ballin has created a sophisticated operation that involves both scientific marvels and criminal activities.

As Deering and Jean work together to unravel the mystery, they find themselves drawn into a web of deception, facing dangerous antagonists in settings ranging from hidden caves to industrial complexes. The Man from Nowhere's true identity and motives remain central mysteries throughout the serial's ten chapters, with revelations that connect to Ballin's scientific ambitions.

The serial climaxes with the truth being revealed—the Man from Nowhere is connected to Ballin's experiments, and the technology at stake could have profound implications for society. Deering must stop the criminal elements while uncovering the genuine scientific purpose behind Ballin's work.
entries.core:
**Early Sound Cinema:** As the first science fiction serial to have full sound, The Voice from the Sky represents a pivotal moment in cinematic history. The serial demonstrates how early filmmakers were beginning to harness sound technology to enhance storytelling in genre films.

**Science and Mystery:** The serial blends the emerging science fiction genre with mystery tropes, creating a hybrid that would influence future serials. The "mad scientist" archetype appears in Edgar Ballin, reflecting contemporary anxieties about unchecked scientific progress.

**The Mysterious Stranger:** The Man from Nowhere archetype taps into a recurring motif of the stranger who arrives to transform a community—echoing religious and folkloric narratives while adding a science fiction twist.

**Lost Film Status:** The serial's classification as a "lost film" adds to its mystique, making it a holy grail for film historians and sci-fi enthusiasts interested in the origins of the genre. Its scarcity has preserved its place in cinematic history as an artifact of early sound-era experimentation.

The serial's combination of science fiction premises with action-adventure serial conventions exemplifies how the genre was evolving in the early sound period, laying groundwork for the wave of sci-fi films that would follow in subsequent decades.

entries.title: The Walking Dead
entries.creator: Michael Curtiz (Director)
entries.pitch: The Walking Dead is a 1936 American horror film starring Boris Karloff in his first non-monster role for Warner Bros. The film centers on John Ellman, a man wrongly convicted of murder and executed, who is brought back to life by a scientist using an experimental serum. While initially appearing to be a miracle of modern science, the film takes a dark turn as Ellman struggles with his altered state and the social stigma surrounding his resurrection. Directed by Michael Curtiz (who would later direct Casablanca), this is one of the earliest films to explore the theme of scientific resurrection and its psychological consequences.
entries.truth:
John Ellman (Boris Karloff) is a man wrongly convicted of murdering his employer and is executed by electric chair. Dr. Edwin Security, a scientist working on an experimental resurrection serum, obtains Ellman's body and successfully revives him. Ellman returns to life, but his face is left scarred and his mental state fragile.

Upon his return to society, Ellman discovers that his resurrection has made him an outcast. His fiancée Marion is horrified by his appearance, and the community fears him as something unnatural. The film explores his struggle to reintegrate into society while dealing with the psychological trauma of death and revival.

Ellman becomes increasingly unstable as he grapples with his identity and place in the world. The film builds to a tragic conclusion that examines whether resurrection is truly a gift or a curse, and whether playing God has dire consequences.
entries.core:
The Walking Dead is notable for being one of the first films to explore the psychological and social consequences of scientific resurrection rather than focusing solely on the spectacle of bringing the dead back to life. While earlier films like Frankenstein dealt with creation of life, this film focuses on what happens after—the emotional and social toll on the resurrected individual.

The film reflects 1930s anxieties about science crossing moral boundaries, the fear of the "other," and questions of identity and humanity. Karloff's performance as a tormented resurrection victim rather than a monster showed his range beyond his famous horror roles and added depth to the genre.

The film also touches on themes of redemption and second chances, though it ultimately presents a pessimistic view—the protagonist cannot escape his fate or find peace, suggesting that death, once occurred, cannot be truly reversed without consequence.

entries.title: The Wandering Earth
entries.creator: Frant Gwo (Director)
entries.pitch: The Wandering Earth is a 2019 Chinese science fiction film directed by Frant Gwo, loosely based on the 2000 short story by Liu Cixin. Set in 2058, the film presents a future where the Sun has begun to expand and will eventually engulf Earth. Under a world government, humanity has constructed 10,000 massive fusion-powered "Earth Engines" to thrust the planet out of the Solar System and migrate to a new home around Alpha Centauri. The film follows astronaut Liu Peiqiang and his son Qi as they fight to save humanity when Earth is thrown off course by Jupiter's gravity. It was China's first full-scale interstellar spectacular and became a massive box office success, grossing over $700 million worldwide.
entries.truth:
In 2058, scientists discover the Sun is anomalously expanding and will consume Earth within 100 years. Under the United Earth Government, humanity begins constructing 10,000 enormous Earth Engines on the Northern Hemisphere to push Earth away from the Sun. Half of humanity is selected by lottery to move underground, while the rest face death from the resulting cataclysms.

Seventeen years later, Liu Peiqiang is about to return to Earth after serving on the International Space Station. His son Qi, now an adult, has obtained thermal suits to take his foster sister Han Duoduo to see the frozen surface of Earth. They are arrested but escape during a massive earthquake caused by Jupiter's gravity affecting Earth's engines.

As Earth approaches Jupiter for a gravity assist, a gravitational spike causes devastating earthquakes that disable many Earth Engines, setting Earth on a collision course with Jupiter. The group joins a rescue mission to transport a Lighter Core to restart an engine in Hangzhou. In the frozen ruins of Shanghai, Qi and his companions lose their vehicle but continue on foot, eventually joining another team working to restart the global engine network.

The final effort requires detonating the engine's blast to push Earth away from Jupiter. Many sacrifice themselves, including Qi and Duoduo's grandfather Han Zi'ang. The combined efforts of rescue teams worldwide succeed in restarting enough engines to push Earth away from Jupiter, saving humanity.
entries.core:
The Wandering Earth represents China's emergence as a major force in big-budget science fiction filmmaking. The film adapts Liu Cixin's short story, transforming it from a more intimate narrative into a grand scale disaster epic. While the original story focused on individual psychological experiences during the migration, the film emphasizes collective human action and sacrifice.

The film explores themes of human unity in the face of existential threats, with the entire world working together under a single government to save humanity. This reflects both optimistic views of global cooperation and serves as a commentary on humanity's potential to transcend national boundaries when faced with annihilation. The father-son relationship between Liu Peiqiang and Qi provides the emotional core, with the younger generation inheriting the responsibility to complete the mission their parents began.

The film also showcases impressive visual effects and production design, depicting a frozen Earth with iconic landmarks like the Great Wall of China and Shanghai Tower encased in ice. While the science has been criticized for impracticality—the scale of the engines relative to Earth, the ability to move a planet, the effects of stopping Earth's rotation—the film prioritizes spectacle and emotional storytelling over hard science. The Wandering Earth launched China's science fiction film renaissance and was followed by a prequel, The Wandering Earth 2, in 2023.

entries.title: The War of the Worlds
entries.creator: H.G. Wells
entries.pitch:
The War of the Worlds is one of the earliest science fiction novels to depict a conflict between humankind and an extraterrestrial race. Written by H.G. Wells between 1895 and 1897 and first published in 1898, it tells the story of an attempted invasion of Earth by beings from Mars with vastly superior intelligence and technology. The Martians intend to eliminate mankind and conquer Earth because their own older, dying world has reached "the last stage of exhaustion."

The novel is told through the first-person perspective of an unnamed protagonist in Surrey and his younger brother, who escapes to Tilingham in Essex as London and Southern England are overrun by the invaders. Rather than focusing on military heroics or technological spectacle, Wells emphasizes the helplessness of ordinary people against an overwhelmingly superior force—a commentary on Victorian era fears of the unknown and anxieties about imperialism and the "other."
entries.truth:
The story begins when a meteor-like object lands on Horsell Common near the narrator's home. It is actually an artificial cylinder from Mars, and several Martians emerge, struggling with Earth's gravity and atmosphere. The alien beings have enormous heads and brains, large eyes, and octopus-like bodies with tentacles. When humans approach the cylinder waving a white flag, the Martians incinerate them with a heat ray.

The next night, the Martians deploy three-legged "fighting-machines" armed with heat rays and chemical weapons. They wipe out the military around the cylinder and destroy Woking. The narrator approaches his home to find his landlord dead and joins forces with an artilleryman whose battery was destroyed. During their escape attempt, the army temporarily destroys a tripod with artillery fire, but soon the Martians attack again with deadly black chemical vapor.

As resistance collapses, refugees flee London. The narrator's brother reaches the coast and boards a refugee ship to Europe. The HMS Thunder Child destroys two tripods before being destroyed itself, allowing the evacuation fleet to escape. Soon, Martians roam unhindered across England.

The narrator is captured but manages to escape. He eventually finds himself hiding near the Martians' landing site, where he witnesses something unexpected: the Martians, despite their technological superiority, begin dying from Earth's bacteria and micro-organisms, to which they have no immunity. The invasion collapses as the Martians perish one by one. Humanity gradually recovers, and the narrator reflects on the humility this encounter should instill in humankind.
entries.core:
The War of the Worlds is significant as one of the earliest stories to detail a conflict between humanity and an extraterrestrial race, establishing many tropes that would become staples of science fiction. The novel reflects Wells's engagement with Darwinian theory—the Martians represent what humanity might become if it continues to evolve while abandoning empathy and compassion. Their view of humans as akin to "beasts that perish" mirrors Social Darwinist anxieties of the era.

The novel has also been interpreted as a commentary on imperialism. Wells was inspired by the catastrophic effect of European colonization on indigenous peoples, particularly the Aboriginal Tasmanians. The Martians' invasion can be read as an inversion of colonial conquest, forcing readers to consider the violence inherent in imperial expansion from the perspective of the conquered.

The 1938 radio broadcast directed by Orson Welles caused widespread panic in the United States, convincing many listeners that a real Martian invasion was underway. This broadcast demonstrated the power of science fiction to reflect contemporary anxieties and showed how speculative narratives could blur the line between fiction and reality.

The novel's enduring legacy lies in its depiction of technological inferiority and the vulnerability of human civilization to forces beyond its comprehension. It established the "alien invasion" genre and influenced countless works from Cold War-era films to modern science fiction exploring first contact and extraterrestrial threats.

entries.title: The War of the Worlds
entries.creator: Byron Haskin
entries.pitch: The War of the Worlds is a landmark 1953 American science fiction film directed by Byron Haskin and produced by George Pal, based on H.G. Wells's 1898 novel. Set in 1950s Southern California instead of Victorian England, the film follows nuclear physicist Dr. Clayton Forrester as he investigates a meteor crash that turns out to be the vanguard of a Martian invasion. Together with Sylvia Van Buren, Forrester witnesses the terrifying power of the Martians' heat-rays and force fields as they systematically devastate military forces and advance toward major cities. The film won the Academy Award for Best Visual Effects and is considered one of the greatest science fiction films of the 1950s.
entries.truth:
The film opens with a prologue warning of the destructive power of modern warfare, then shifts to a small California town where a meteor lands near Linda Rosa. Dr. Clayton Forrester, a nuclear physicist, suspects the object is hollow and investigates alongside Sylvia Van Buren and her uncle, Pastor Collins. When the Martians emerge from their craft, they annihilate three soldiers with a heat-ray weapon. More meteors land worldwide as the military mobilizes, but conventional weapons prove useless against the Martians' invulnerable force fields.

As the invasion escalates, the Martians deploy fighting machines equipped with heat-rays and black smoke, destroying everything in their path. Forrester and Sylvia survive the initial attacks and flee with the military, witnessing the complete collapse of human civilization's defenses. The film reaches its climax as the Martians seem poised to conquer Earth—only for them to mysteriously die from exposure to Earth's common bacteria, to which they have no immunity. In the final scene, Forrester and Sylvia emerge from hiding to find the Martian machines silent and the surviving humans捡回一条命.
entries.core:
The War of the Worlds (1953) represents a crucial moment in science fiction cinema, bringing the techniques of George Pal's production to bear on classic Wellsian themes. The film translates Victorian England's invasion terror to Cold War America, replacing the stationary Martians of the novel with mobile fighting machines that evoke tanks and war machinery—reflecting contemporary anxieties about technological warfare and atomic weapons.

The film's greatest achievement is its visual effects, which earned the Academy Award for Best Visual Effects. The Martian machines, designed by R. A.烟火, combine organic and mechanical elements in genuinely unsettling ways. The iconic image of the tripod-like fighting machines striding across the California landscape, their heat-rays incandescent against the night sky, created visual vocabulary that influenced decades of science fiction imagery.

Thematically, the film reflects 1950s Cold War anxieties about nuclear annihilation and technological superiority. The Martians represent an unfathomable, unstoppable enemy—much like fears of Soviet communism or nuclear war. Yet the film's resolution offers a nuanced message: humanity is saved not by its military might or intellectual superiority but by simple biological accident. Earth's microbes, utterly insignificant to the advanced Martians, prove to be their Achilles heel. This suggests a humbling perspective on human significance in the cosmos—one that would be explored in subsequent alien invasion narratives.

The 1953 War of the Worlds also established template elements for the alien invasion genre: the initial crash/landing, the heat-ray weapon, the military's futile resistance, the survival protagonists, and the unexpected biological salvation. These elements would be revisited and varied in countless films to follow, making this a foundational text for science fiction cinema.

entries.title: The War of the Worlds: Next Century
entries.creator: Piotr Szulkin (Director/Writer)
entries.pitch: In a dystopian near-future Poland, aliens from Mars arrive with ostensibly friendly intentions, but their presence quickly transforms Earth into a totalitarian police state where television serves as the primary tool of propaganda and control. The film follows Iron Idem, a news presenter for the state-controlled television network, who finds himself complicit in the alien occupation as he is forced to broadcast pro-alien messaging to the masses. When his wife is kidnapped and he witnesses the brutal repression of citizens, Idem must navigate his moral awakening and decide whether to remain a puppet of the regime or risk everything to rebel against both the alien invaders and their human collaborators.
entries.truth:
The film opens with the arrival of Martians on Earth, who present themselves as benevolent visitors seeking friendship with humanity. However, their presence immediately transforms the visited location into a surreal police state where television becomes the dominant tool of propaganda and social control.

The protagonist, Iron Idem (played by Roman Wilhelmi), is a news presenter for the TV program "Iron Idem's Independent News" – a program that, despite its name, is carefully controlled by his superiors. Once the aliens arrive, Idem is pressured to shift his programming to overtly support the Martian presence, becoming an unwilling mouthpiece for the occupation.

One morning, Idem wakes to find police officers and aliens ransacking his apartment. His wife is kidnapped in a body bag, and Idem is forced to collaborate further with the state apparatus, which now works alongside the Martians to encourage citizens to donate blood – a seemingly benign request that carries darker implications.

After being thrown out of his apartment, Idem wanders through the city, observing citizens who have become stupefied and apathetic in the face of repression. He eventually decides to rebel during a televised event called "TV Super Show" – a concert organized as a farewell to the Martians. During this broadcast, Idem delivers a scathing critique of the aliens and the state response to their presence.

However, Idem's rebellion is misinterpreted by the public. When he sits at an outdoor market afterward, a water supply worker recognizes him and tells him he "failed to pull it off," noting that society is content with what the government provides: "everything under a single pillar, a healthy climate, beer available." Disillusioned by this response, Idem removes his wig and gives it to the worker's child, telling the boy that tomorrow he will take Idem's place.

Later, an old man who had been trying to destroy televisions approaches Idem and confesses he plans to blow up the local TV station, asking for Idem's help due to his special access. Suspicious, Idem warns this sounds like a setup – which it is. Government workers pull Idem into a car and force him to watch as the old man is blown up. They assure Idem the man will become a martyr, memorialized in statue form.

After the Martians depart, Earth's media completely reverses its narrative. The alien visit is now depicted as an aggressive invasion, and Iron Idem is painted as the main collaborator. Idem is arrested and put on trial. In exchange for seeing his wife one last time, Idem agrees to play up his sensational television character until his execution.

Idem's former employer agrees to this arrangement, and his wife is delivered to him – dead in the same bag her captors used to kidnap her. Idem is sentenced to death by firing squad and tied to a pole while a television screen indicates the execution is being filmed for public consumption. However, when the firing squad shoots, only the version of Idem on the television screen collapses. The real Idem, still tied to the pole, remains unscathed and conscious, appearing amazed at his survival. He frees himself and leaves the television studio, stepping into an outside world obfuscated by light and mist.
entries.core:
The War of the Worlds: Next Century is a razor-sharp satirical allegory that functions on multiple levels. Most obviously, it's a critique of totalitarian states and the manipulation of media for propaganda purposes – particularly pointed given it was made in Communist Poland in 1981, just before martial law was declared.

The film explores how television specifically functions as a tool of social control, creating false narratives and shaping public perception. The protagonist's journey from compliant broadcaster to reluctant rebel to scapegoated traitor illustrates the moral compromises individuals make under authoritarian regimes, and how those who collaborate – even reluctantly – become expendable when political winds shift.

Szulkin inverts H.G. Wells' original narrative: instead of alien invasion as external threat, the Martians are initially welcomed, and the true horror comes from the human collaboration and the system that enables occupation. The blood donation request echoes historical atrocities while appearing superficially benign.

The film's ambigious ending – where Idem survives his execution both literally (the bullets miss) and figuratively (he becomes a television ghost) – suggests that in a world dominated by mediated reality, the distinction between representation and reality has completely collapsed. The "real" Idem can only exist outside the frame, in the misty world beyond the television screen.

Ultimately, the film asks uncomfortable questions about complicity, resistance, and whether individual rebellion can ever truly matter when the systems of power are so thoroughly entrenched.

entries.title: The Whispering Star
entries.creator: Sion Sono (Director)
entries.pitch: The Whispering Star is a 2015 Japanese science fiction film directed by Sion Sono, serving as his first venture into the sci-fi genre. The film follows Yoko Suzuki, an A.I. cyborg working as a delivery woman in an unspecified future where humanity has scattered across the galaxy to various earth-like planets, each showing clear signs of environmental disaster. The film is notable for being shot almost entirely in black-and-white, creating a stark, meditative atmosphere. Through her encounters with the delivery recipients, Yoko learns to understand and appreciate human emotions, leading to a profound journey of self-discovery. The film premiered at the 2015 Toronto International Film Festival and won the NETPAC Award for World or International Asian Film Premiere.
entries.truth:
Set in an unspecified future, humanity has spread across the galaxy, with survivors living on different earth-like planets that show clear signs of environmental disasters. Yoko Suzuki is an A.I. cyborg delivery woman who travels through space delivering packages to the few humans still remaining. Her work takes her from one desolate world to another, where she encounters various recipients dealing with the aftermath of ecological collapse.

Throughout her deliveries, Yoko interacts with humans who represent different aspects of emotion and experience that she, as an artificial intelligence, does not naturally possess. These encounters gradually begin to change her, as she witnesses grief, joy, love, and loss among the humans she serves. The film explores her growing awareness and the development of what might be called machine consciousness or emotional understanding.

The narrative is deliberately slow-paced and contemplative, reflecting Sono's characteristic style. The black-and-white cinematography emphasizes the isolation and desolation of the post-apocalyptic setting, while also creating a stark beauty. The film's episodic structure follows Yoko from planet to planet, with each delivery offering a glimpse into different human experiences and emotions.

As Yoko continues her deliveries, she begins to question her own existence and purpose, leading to a transformation in her understanding of what it means to be alive. The film culminates in her emotional awakening, even as the environmental devastation of the human worlds continues unchecked around her.
entries.core:
**Artificial Intelligence and Consciousness:** The film explores the question of whether an A.I. can develop genuine emotions and understanding of human experience. Yoko's journey from a purely functional delivery mechanism to a being capable of appreciating emotional complexity forms the emotional core of the film. Sono examines whether consciousness can emerge from programmed tasks and whether artificial beings can truly understand concepts like loneliness, grief, and joy.

**Environmental Apocalypse:** The Whispering Star presents a vision of near-future Earth where environmental disasters have forced humanity to scatter across the galaxy. Each planet Yoko visits shows the aftermath of ecological collapse—wasted landscapes, abandoned structures, and small pockets of surviving humans. The film serves as an environmental meditation, questioning humanity's relationship with nature and the consequences of ecological destruction.

**Isolation and Connection:** Despite the vastness of space, the film emphasizes the profound isolation of both humans and A.I.s. Each character Yoko encounters is isolated in their own way—by distance, by loss, by the collapse of civilization. The film explores how connection can emerge even in the most isolated circumstances, and how the act of delivery—bringing something from one place to another—can create meaningful bonds.

**Sion Sono's Directorial Vision:** The Whispering Star represents Sono's first sci-fi film and his first production through his own company, Sion Production. The film was shot primarily in Fukushima Prefecture, adding layers of meaning given the region's real-world environmental history. Sono's signature long takes and meditative pacing create a unique sci-fi experience that prioritizes atmosphere and introspection over conventional narrative momentum.

The film stands as a unique entry in the sci-fi canon—a quiet, philosophical meditation on consciousness, environment, and what it means to be human in a dying universe.

entries.title: The Witches of Karres
entries.creator: James H. Schmitz
entries.pitch: Captain Pausert is an inexperienced merchant traveler on a solo voyage through space, piloting an old pirate chaser called the Venture. While stopping at the planet Porlumma, he witnesses three young sisters—Maleen, Goth, and the Leewit—being sold as slaves. Moved by sympathy, he purchases their freedom and helps them escape. But these aren't ordinary children: they're witches from the forbidden planet Karres, possessing powerful psionic abilities and a revolutionary faster-than-light drive called the Sheewash Drive. What begins as an act of compassion thrusts Pausert into a galaxy-spanning adventure involving interdimensional alien invaders, space pirates, and governments desperate to capture the secret of the witches' incredible technology. This is a rollicking space opera with humor, heart, and plenty of action—lighter in tone than typical science fiction of its era.
entries.truth:
The story opens with Captain Pausert, a young merchant traveler from the planet Nikkeldepain, voyaging solo aboard the Venture. While on Porlumma, he encounters three young girls—Maleen (about 14), Goth (9 or 10), and the Leewit (5 or 6)—who had been enslaved after their own spacecraft crashed during a pleasure trip. Moved by their plight, Pausert uses his limited funds to purchase their freedom.

During the escape from Porlumma, the girls desperately engage what they call the Sheewash Drive, enabling the Venture to outrun pursuit at speeds impossible for conventional space drives. Pausert learns the girls are witches from Karres—a prohibited planet—possessing powerful psychic abilities known as klatha. Their psionic powers and especially the impossibly fast Sheewash Drive attract dangerous attention from various planetary governments and the Imperium itself.

Pausert takes the sisters to their homeworld of Karres, only to find himself facing a barrage of criminal charges upon returning to Nikkeldepain—many relating to his association with the witches and his visit to the forbidden planet. The planetary government is desperate to obtain the suspected new space drive technology.

With the help of Goth, who stowed away on the Venture, Pausert escapes Nikkeldepain and heads to Uldune—a former pirate stronghold now serving as a galactic marketplace where anything can be bought or sold. They rebuild the ship under a new name, "Evening Bird," and assume new identities. During this time, Pausert begins developing minor klatha powers of his own.

Their new trading venture draws the attention of both planetary authorities and the Imperium. They run afoul of industrial espionage, kidnapping attempts, and political intrigue. The ruler of Uldune, believing both Pausert and Goth are witches, entrusts them with a mysterious payload: a frightening rocky mass and a catatonic witch found with it.

The voyage becomes increasingly strange as fellow crew members Vezzarn and Hulik do Eldel are revealed to be spies attempting to locate the Sheewash Drive. Vezzarn uncovers the mystery mass, attracting dangerous alien attention. The situation escalates as they navigate the treacherous Chaladoor region of space, with the mysterious payload playing a key role in the eventual resolution.

Through cleverness, courage, and the witches' psionic powers, Pausert and his companions ultimately triumph over their pursuers and the interdimensional threats. The story concludes with the protagonists having proven themselves against overwhelming odds while forging unexpected bonds of loyalty and friendship.
entries.core:
The Witches of Karres stands out as a remarkable piece of science fiction for several reasons. First, Schmitz presents a more optimistic, tolerant universe than was commons space opera. His galaxies are places in 1960 where humans and aliens can coexist, where ordinary people can live viable lives beyond the constant threat of existential monsters.

The novel features remarkably progressive characterization for its era. Schmitz was decades ahead of the curve in portraying strong female characters. The witches—particularly Goth—are competent, resourceful, and completely equal to their male counterparts without relying on sexual role-playing clichés. This was groundbreaking at a time when female characters in adventure fiction were typically relegated to damsel-in-distress roles.

The story explores themes of freedom versus oppression, as the witches escape slavery and must defend their autonomy against governments and corporations seeking to exploit their abilities and technology. It also examines the ethics of power—both technological (the Sheewash Drive) and psionic (klatha)—and the responsibility that comes with possessing such advantages.

Schmitz's tolerance and sympathy extend even to potential antagonists. His aliens and monsters are often revealed to have their own agendas and perspectives from which their actions are justified or even admirable. This nuanced view of "the other" was almost unique in space opera of the period, which was typically xenophobic.

The novel's lighter tone and humor distinguish it from the often grim and pessimistic science fiction of its era. Rather than exploring dystopian futures or existential dread, The Witches of Karres offers pure adventure—escapism in the best sense, a reminder that science fiction could be fun, optimistic, and entertainment-oriented without sacrificing substance.

The Witches of Karres was nominated for a Hugo Award and remains thought of as Schmitz's best work. It helped establish him as a master of space opera who could combine action, humor, and thoughtful characterization in ways that influenced later writers.

entries.title: The Word for World is Forest
entries.pitch:
On the forest world of Athshe, a peaceful civilization of small, gentle humans has lived in harmony with their environment for millennia. When Earth colonists arrive to exploit the planet's valuable timber resources, they bring with them brutal technology and an assumption of superiority. The Athsheans' word for "world" is "forest"—they cannot conceive of existence without the green world that sustains them. But the colonists see only profit, and they begin clear-cutting the sacred woods with no regard for the ecological or spiritual devastation left in their wake.

What begins as exploitation escalates into massacre when the colonists' leader, a man called the Captain, orders the systematic destruction of Athshean villages after a failed rebellion. But the Athsheans possess a secret weapon: their dreams are shared, their consciousness collective, and their capacity for violence—once awakened—cannot be easily contained. This is a brutal meditation on how the oppressed become the oppressor, and how violence begets violence in an endless cycle.
entries.truth:
The novella is set on Athshe, a planet where the indigenous humanoid population has developed a unique culture centered on their forest environment. For the Athsheans, the forest is not merely home—it is the source of their spirituality, their identity, and their very language. Their word for "world" and "forest" is the same. They are a gentle people who resolve conflict through shared dreaming and have no concept of war.

Earth, in Le Guin's future, is overpopulated and desperate for resources. The Company sends colonists to Athshe to harvest the valuable timber that covers most of the planet. The colonists are a mix of desperate poor people and Company representatives, and they establish a small settlement surrounded by the endless forest.

The Captain, the leader of the colonists, is a brutal man who sees the Athsheans as subhuman—mere "creatures" to be used and discarded.
